title: API Authentication Flaws
text:
API Authentication Flaws 
 Definition 
 API authentication flaws are practical weaknesses in how an API verifies identity across clients, tokens, devices, and workflows. This note focuses on the applied failure patterns beyond the broader category label. 
 Why it matters 
 Authentication in APIs is rarely just one login form. It involves mobile apps, SPAs, machine clients, refresh flows, service tokens, and recovery logic. Complexity creates inconsistency.
This note is narrower than  broken-authentication : it focuses on how those inconsistencies appear in real API flows, while  jwt-attacks  and  token-lifecycle  isolate token-specific failure modes. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- differences between clients
- incomplete MFA enforcement
- password reset and device flows
- token bridging between auth stages
- machine credentials with excessive reach 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- document all auth entry points and token types
- keep auth logic centralized where possible
- ensure every client follows equivalent security guarantees
- test recovery and device flows as thoroughly as login 
 Practical examples 
 
 a mobile API route skips the same control enforced in the web flow 
 device registration creates long-lived credentials with weak revocation 
 password reset tokens can be replayed or abused 
 
 Related notes 
 
  broken-authentication  
  jwt-attacks  
  token-lifecycle  
  Break JWT Validation  
  Auth Flaws  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet — https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html 
  Foundational:  OWASP API2:2023 Broken Authentication — https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa2-broken-authentication/

title: API Inventory Management
text:
API Inventory Management 
 Definition 
 API inventory management is the practice of knowing which API versions, hosts, routes, schemas, and environments exist, are reachable, and are still supposed to exist. 
 Why it matters 
 A lot of API risk is not a single vulnerability but exposure drift. Old versions, undocumented endpoints, internal APIs, forgotten hosts, and test environments create attack surface long after teams stop thinking about them.
For APIs, this is tightly tied to attack-surface mapping: machine clients can keep calling deprecated or hidden routes even after the product UI stops using them. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- deprecated versions
- hidden or undocumented endpoints
- mismatches between frontend usage and backend availability
- staging, internal, or legacy hosts
- schema or documentation leakage 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- maintain an API inventory as part of security and release process
- retire old versions intentionally
- track environment exposure
- review what is externally reachable, not just what is documented 
 Practical examples 
 
  /api/v1/  is still reachable after the app migrated to v2 
 mobile APIs expose routes the web client never calls 
 staging endpoints are internet-facing and forgotten

  Endpoint Discovery  
  External Attack Surface  
  HTTP Overview

title: API Rate Limiting
text:
API Rate Limiting 
 Definition 
 API rate limiting is the set of controls that restrict how often a client, identity, token, or source can consume API resources within a period. 
 Why it matters 
 In APIs, abuse is often automated. Weak limits can allow credential attacks, scraping, brute-force enumeration, cost spikes, and denial-of-wallet style issues even when no classic vulnerability exists.
Because API clients can call routes directly without UI pacing, rate limiting is about abuse control and resilience as much as brute-force prevention. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- request burst tolerance
- per-IP vs per-account vs per-token enforcement
- whether limits vary across routes or versions
- whether alternate clients or headers bypass limits 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- define what resource is being protected
- apply limits at the right identity and route scopes
- treat rate limiting as both abuse control and resilience control
- monitor evasion and saturation behavior 
 Practical examples 
 
 login endpoints allow unlimited credential attempts 
 search endpoints can be scraped at high volume 
 expensive report generation lacks meaningful control

  api-inventory-management

title: API Security Top 10
text:
API Security Top 10 
 Definition 
 The OWASP API Security Top 10 is a focused awareness framework for the most important API-specific security risk categories. It complements, rather than replaces, broader web-security guidance. 
 Why it matters 
 API security has its own failure patterns. APIs expose object identifiers, machine-readable data, business functions, and backend integration surfaces in ways that are often broader than a traditional UI. This changes what attackers enumerate and how defenders should think about trust boundaries. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use APIs because:
- the surface is often larger than the web UI exposes
- object identifiers are easier to manipulate directly
- functions are callable without the frontend
- machine-readable responses are easier to diff and automate against
- undocumented or legacy endpoints often remain reachable 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use the API Top 10 to:
- prioritize common API failure modes
- distinguish object-, function-, and property-level authorization
- connect backend implementation choices to exposure
- design safer API review and testing workflows 
 Practical examples 
 
 The UI hides fields the API still returns. 
 Admin-only actions are callable directly by a normal client. 
 Old API versions remain deployed and discoverable. 
 
 Related notes 
 
  authorization  
  broken-object-level-authorization  
  broken-authentication  
  api-inventory-management  
 
 References

title: Authorization
text:
Authorization 
 Definition 
 Authorization is the process of determining what an authenticated identity is allowed to access or do. In APIs, that includes object-level access, function-level permissions, and field/property visibility or mutability. 
 Why it matters 
 A lot of teams implement authentication and then assume authorization follows automatically. It does not. In APIs especially, the client can call endpoints directly, manipulate identifiers, and send fields that the UI never exposes.
This note is the umbrella for API-side access decisions; the broken-* notes below describe how that failure shows up at object, function, and property boundaries. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test authorization by changing:
- object identifiers
- route families
- roles and privilege assumptions
- hidden parameters and fields
- call order and workflow stages 
 They care less about what the frontend allows and more about what the server accepts. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- treat authorization as a first-class server-side concern
- define policy at object, function, and property levels
- deny by default
- test authorization independently from authentication and UI behavior 
 Practical examples 
 
 A user is authenticated but can read another user's invoice. 
 A normal client can call an admin-only function directly. 
 An API accepts a restricted field in JSON and updates it. 
 
 Related notes

  broken-function-level-authorization  
  broken-object-property-level-authorization

title: Broken Authentication
text:
Broken Authentication 
 Definition 
 Broken authentication in APIs refers to weaknesses in identity verification, credential handling, token issuance, or authentication workflow enforcement that let attackers gain or maintain unauthorized access. 
 Why it matters 
 APIs are often consumed by multiple clients and services, which increases the chance of inconsistent authentication assumptions. A flaw in token issuance, MFA handling, session bridging, or account recovery can affect every consumer.
This note keeps the category-level view;  api-auth-flaws  focuses on applied workflow inconsistencies, while  jwt-attacks  and  token-lifecycle  cover token-specific trust failures. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- login and account recovery flows
- token issuance before full verification
- weak MFA or device binding
- reuse of stale or revoked credentials
- differences between web, mobile, and machine clients 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- make authentication rules consistent across all clients
- treat token issuance as a sensitive security boundary
- harden recovery and MFA flows
- separate “has some credential” from “is fully authorized now” 
 Practical examples 
 
 an API issues a token before MFA is completed 
 logout does not revoke API usability in practice 
 mobile and web flows validate credentials differently 
 
 Related notes

  jwt-attacks  
  token-lifecycle  
  Auth Flaws

title: Broken Function Level Authorization
text:
Broken Function Level Authorization 
 Definition 
 Broken Function Level Authorization (BFLA) happens when an API exposes a function or action that a client should not be allowed to invoke, often because route-level privilege checks are missing or inconsistent. 
 Why it matters 
 APIs often expose business functions directly. When those functions are callable without the intended role or privilege level, impact can be severe even if object IDs are not involved.
This is different from BOLA and BOPLA: the problem here is invoking the wrong action at all, not just touching the wrong object or field. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test BFLA by:
- browsing undocumented or role-specific routes
- changing methods, paths, or route families
- calling admin workflows directly
- comparing frontend behavior with raw API capability 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- enforce action-level authorization server-side
- review every privileged function independently
- avoid relying on hidden buttons or route obscurity
- document which roles can call which actions 
 Practical examples 
 
 a normal user can hit an admin export endpoint 
 a read-only user can call a delete action directly 
 an internal moderation endpoint is reachable from the public app 
 
 Related notes

  broken-object-level-authorization  
  broken-object-property-level-authorization  
  Broken Access Control

title: Broken Object Level Authorization
text:
Broken Object Level Authorization 
 Definition 
 Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) happens when an API lets a client access or manipulate an object it should not be able to access, usually by changing an identifier. 
 Why it matters 
 This is one of the most common and high-impact API risk classes because APIs naturally expose object identifiers. If the server does not check ownership or authorization every time an object is accessed, unauthorized access follows quickly.
It is closely related to web IDOR, but the API framing matters because machine clients can call object routes directly and automate cross-object testing at scale. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test for BOLA by:
- changing IDs in routes, params, or bodies
- replaying requests from one user against another user's object
- enumerating predictable IDs or leaked references
- testing both read and write actions 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- enforce authorization on every object lookup
- avoid assuming UUIDs alone solve the problem
- log suspicious cross-object access patterns
- separate object ownership from UI visibility 
 Practical examples 
 
  /api/orders/101  becomes  /api/orders/102  
 a mobile client exposes object IDs the web UI never shows 
 a PATCH request updates another user's resource by changing the ID 
 
 Related notes 
 
  authorization  
  IDOR  
  Exploit IDOR  
  api-security-top-10  
  HTTP Messages  
 
 References

title: Broken Object Property Level Authorization
text:
Broken Object Property Level Authorization 
 Definition 
 Broken Object Property Level Authorization (BOPLA) happens when an API does not correctly enforce which object properties a client can read or modify. 
 Why it matters 
 This risk explains why APIs can be dangerous even when route-level authorization appears correct. The endpoint may be allowed, but the fields within the response or request are not properly constrained.
Keep the boundary tight:  excessive-data-exposure  is the read-side symptom, while  mass-assignment  is the write-side symptom. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- whether hidden or sensitive fields are returned
- whether writable fields exist that the UI never exposes
- whether property-level differences exist between roles
- whether JSON structures can include extra fields safely 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- define which properties are readable and writable by role and context
- avoid over-serializing internal objects
- validate allowed fields explicitly
- review serializers, DTOs, and binding helpers carefully 
 Practical examples 
 
 the API returns internal IDs, flags, or PII the UI never shows 
 the request accepts a property like  isAdmin  or  creditLimit  
 different roles should see different fields, but the server returns all of them 
 
 Related notes 
 
  excessive-data-exposure  
  mass-assignment  
  authorization  
  api-security-top-10  
 
 References

title: Excessive Data Exposure
text:
Excessive Data Exposure 
 Definition 
 Excessive data exposure happens when an API returns more data than the client actually needs, often trusting the frontend to hide or ignore sensitive fields. 
 Why it matters 
 APIs are machine-readable, so extra fields are easy to inspect and diff. Even if the UI never renders them, they may still expose internal identifiers, secrets, personal data, or business-sensitive state.
This is primarily a read-side problem: the server returns too much.  mass-assignment  is the corresponding write-side risk where the server accepts too much. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers compare:
- what the UI displays
- what the raw response contains
- what different roles receive
- what changes across endpoints and versions 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- return only fields the client needs
- define DTOs intentionally instead of serializing internal models
- review role-based field visibility
- test raw responses, not just UI output 
 Practical examples 
 
 the response includes password hashes, internal IDs, or IP addresses 
 an admin-only field appears for normal users but is simply hidden in the UI 
 old API versions still return verbose internal objects 
 
 Related notes

title: JWT Attacks
text:
JWT Attacks 
 Definition 
 JWT attacks are weaknesses in how JSON Web Tokens are issued, validated, trusted, scoped, or expired. The risk is rarely “JWT exists”; the risk is how the application uses it. 
 Why it matters 
 JWTs are attractive because they are portable and easy to distribute across clients and services. But they also encourage teams to push trust decisions into claims that can become stale, inconsistent, or insufficiently validated.
This note is specifically about token validation and token-contained trust, not every authentication flaw that happens to use JWTs. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- expired token acceptance
- weak or inconsistent claim validation
- stale roles or scopes inside tokens
- differences across services consuming the same token
- assumptions that token presence implies authorization 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- validate signature and critical claims consistently
- avoid overloading tokens with long-lived authorization truth
- keep token lifetime and scope narrow
- design revocation and rotation intentionally
- separate authentication from authorization checks 
 Practical examples 
 
 one service rejects an expired token while another still accepts it 
 a role change is not reflected because old tokens remain valid 
 a token with valid structure but wrong audience is still accepted 
 
 Related notes 
 
  broken-authentication  
  api-auth-flaws  
  token-lifecycle  
  Break JWT Validation

title: Mass Assignment
text:
Mass Assignment 
 Definition 
 Mass assignment happens when an API automatically binds client-supplied fields onto an internal object without restricting which properties can be set. 
 Why it matters 
 This is one of the most dangerous “framework convenience” risks. APIs often accept JSON bodies that look harmless, but binding helpers may accept fields the product never intended users to control.
This is mainly a write-side problem: the client can set properties it should never control.  excessive-data-exposure  covers the read-side version of overexposed object data. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- extra JSON properties
- hidden fields from admin or internal flows
- guessed boolean/role/ownership properties
- differences between create and update endpoints 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- use explicit allowlists for bindable fields
- avoid binding directly to domain entities
- separate public request DTOs from internal models
- review framework defaults and helper behavior 
 Practical examples 
 
 sending  {"isAdmin": true}  is accepted 
 update endpoints allow changing ownership or approval state 
 internal-only fields are mutable because the framework auto-binds them 
 
 Related notes 
 
  broken-object-property-level-authorization  
  excessive-data-exposure  
  authorization  
  api-security-top-10  
 
 References

  Foundational:  OWASP API3:2023 Broken Object Property Level Authorization — https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa3-bopla/

title: Token Lifecycle
text:
Token Lifecycle 
 Definition 
 Token lifecycle refers to how tokens are created, issued, refreshed, rotated, revoked, expired, and invalidated across the life of an authenticated API session or machine relationship. 
 Why it matters 
 Many API authentication weaknesses are lifecycle problems rather than login problems. Tokens that are too long-lived, insufficiently rotated, or poorly revoked become standing trust artifacts.
This note is about what happens after issuance;  jwt-attacks  is the better fit for validation semantics, while  broken-authentication  and  api-auth-flaws  stay broader. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- refresh tokens that never rotate
- access tokens that remain valid after logout or password change
- tokens that outlive privilege changes
- inconsistent revocation across services 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- define clear TTLs and trust transitions
- rotate refresh tokens intentionally
- revoke tokens on meaningful account and security events
- avoid keeping stale authority embedded for too long 
 Practical examples 
 
 access tokens remain valid after the user changes password 
 refresh token rotation is missing, enabling replay 
 role downgrade does not affect already-issued tokens

title: Admin Interface Discovery
text:
Admin Interface Discovery 
 Definition 
 Admin interface discovery is the process of identifying management, control-plane, support, diagnostic, or privileged interfaces that should be restricted but may still be reachable. 
 Why it matters 
 Admin surfaces often have high impact because they expose privileged functionality. They are also frequently forgotten, hidden behind non-obvious hosts, or protected only by weak assumptions like obscurity or network location.
Keep the scope narrow: this note is about discovering privileged or control-plane surfaces, not generic route discovery across the whole application. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- admin subdomains
-  /admin ,  /manage ,  /console ,  /debug  paths
- alternate virtual hosts
- health and support panels
- vendor dashboards and default management endpoints 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- inventory admin surfaces explicitly
- restrict them by strong auth and exposure policy
- separate admin and public paths where possible
- review whether “internal only” really holds in practice 
 Practical examples 
 
 a management console is reachable on a forgotten hostname 
 a support/debug panel is left exposed after incident troubleshooting 
 a vendor appliance exposes a public admin interface

title: Attack Surface Mapping
text:
Attack Surface Mapping 
 Definition 
 Attack surface mapping is the practice of identifying what assets, services, routes, interfaces, dependencies, and environments are actually exposed or reachable from an attacker’s perspective. 
 Why it matters 
 Security programs often reason from architecture diagrams, intended designs, or ownership assumptions. Attack surface mapping starts from observable reality instead. It is how you detect forgotten services, stale DNS, undocumented APIs, alternate entry points, and vendor-connected exposure.
This is the umbrella note for the branch:  external-attack-surface  and  internal-attack-surface  narrow that model to where the exposure is reachable from. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers do not start with your documentation. They start with:
- domains and subdomains
- ports and protocols
- web routes and APIs
- admin interfaces
- old versions
- exposed storage
- third-party services and trust relationships 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use attack surface mapping to:
- compare expected exposure vs real exposure
- prioritize internet-facing risk
- find drift across environments and versions
- support inventory, ownership, and retirement work 
 Practical examples 
 
 a deprecated API version is still publicly reachable 
 a forgotten admin panel exists on a subdomain not in the main docs 
 a third-party host is still trusted after the vendor integration changed 
 
 Related notes

  internal-attack-surface  
  API Inventory Management

title: Deprecated API Versions
text:
Deprecated API Versions 
 Definition 
 Deprecated API versions are old or superseded endpoints that remain reachable after the organization believes clients have moved on. 
 Why it matters 
 Old API versions often preserve weaker auth, noisier responses, looser validation, or simpler business logic. They create a time-shifted attack surface where older assumptions remain exploitable even after the main product has improved.
This is usually an inventory-drift problem first and a vulnerability category second: the old surface remains reachable because retirement lagged behind delivery. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
-  /v1 ,  /beta ,  /legacy , or hidden version routes
- mobile-only or client-specific versions
- old schemas and docs still in circulation
- differences in auth, rate limiting, and field exposure between versions 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- track versions as part of inventory
- decommission old versions deliberately
- compare old and new control sets
- monitor traffic to deprecated versions and treat it as exposure until removed 
 Practical examples 
 
  /api/v1/  still returns more fields than  /api/v2/  
 a deprecated mobile API path remains internet-facing 
 old auth or role checks persist in a legacy version 
 
 Related notes

  endpoint-discovery  
  Excessive Data Exposure  
  external-attack-surface  
 
 References

title: Endpoint Discovery
text:
Endpoint Discovery 
 Definition 
 Endpoint discovery is the process of finding reachable routes, APIs, handlers, and hidden request surfaces beyond what the visible UI or official docs reveal. 
 Why it matters 
 Applications and APIs often expose more routes than the frontend uses. Old versions, hidden handlers, undocumented API endpoints, and JS-discovered routes all expand the true attack surface.
This is where frontend/backend mismatch becomes operational: clients can call machine-readable routes directly even when the UI no longer exposes them. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers discover endpoints through:
- crawling and spidering
- JavaScript analysis
- archived URLs and historical sources
- API schemas, docs, and client apps
- guessing route families and versions 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- know which endpoints are real and supported
- remove or retire dead routes
- compare frontend usage to backend availability
- review endpoint discovery as part of inventory management 
 Practical examples 
 
 a JavaScript bundle exposes undocumented API routes 
 an old  /v1/  path still works after migration to  /v2/  
 mobile clients call endpoints the web app never touches

title: Exposed Storage
text:
Exposed Storage 
 Definition 
 Exposed storage is any file or object storage surface that is reachable or readable in a way that exceeds its intended audience, whether through direct public access, weak policy, predictable paths, or inherited trust. 
 Why it matters 
 Storage exposure often becomes a silent data leak rather than a dramatic exploit. Backups, artifacts, logs, build outputs, uploaded files, and internal documents frequently end up in locations that were easy to publish but never properly governed.
Keep it distinct from  File Upload Abuse : this note is about storage itself as exposed surface, not the application logic that accepted the file. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- public object storage buckets/containers
- predictable file paths
- exposed backups and artifacts
- uploads stored in publicly browsable paths
- artifacts linked from CI/CD or staging 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- inventory storage surfaces as part of attack surface
- review policy, listing behavior, and indirect exposure
- separate public content from private content aggressively
- tie storage reviews to build and release processes 
 Practical examples 
 
 a public bucket exposes build artifacts or source maps 
 uploaded files are publicly accessible even when they should be private 
 a backup archive is reachable by predictable URL 
 
 Related notes

  deprecated-api-versions

  Public Asset Discovery  
 
 References

title: External Attack Surface
text:
External Attack Surface 
 Definition 
 External attack surface is the set of assets and entry points reachable from outside the organization’s internal networks, especially over the internet. 
 Why it matters 
 This is usually the first surface real attackers see. It includes public hosts, domains, APIs, ports, exposed storage, remote access services, and vendor-connected interfaces. If it is reachable externally, it becomes part of the prioritization problem.
Keep it distinct from  internal-attack-surface : external surface is about internet-reachable exposure, not what becomes reachable only after a foothold or SSRF path. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers enumerate:
- root domains and subdomains
- IPs and ASNs
- internet-facing services and ports
- public web apps and APIs
- exposed dashboards, storage, and login surfaces 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- know what is internet-facing
- continuously compare intended vs actual exposure
- reduce unnecessary public entry points
- treat discovery and retirement as ongoing processes 
 Practical examples 
 
 a staging host is publicly reachable 
 an old VPN or remote admin surface is still online 
 public object storage exposes sensitive artifacts

title: Internal Attack Surface
text:
Internal Attack Surface 
 Definition 
 Internal attack surface is the set of services, routes, networks, metadata endpoints, admin interfaces, and trust paths that are reachable from inside an environment even if they are not public. 
 Why it matters 
 Many severe incidents come from the gap between public reachability and internal reachability. Once an attacker gains server-side execution, SSRF capability, or a foothold in one workload, internal-only services become part of the real surface.
That makes this note different from  external-attack-surface : the core question here is what becomes reachable after trust-boundary collapse, not what is already internet-facing. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- metadata endpoints
- internal admin APIs
- private network services
- weakly segmented east-west paths
- services that trust internal callers too much 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- know what workloads can reach internally
- treat internal-only as a smaller trust boundary, not a safe state
- map reachability from application nodes, not just from laptops
- review how SSRF and proxies change internal exposure 
 Practical examples 
 
 an app server can reach cloud metadata and internal admin APIs 
 a supposedly private service is exposed to every workload in a VPC 
 internal dashboards rely only on network location, not auth 
 
 Related notes

  Metadata Endpoints

title: Service Enumeration
text:
Service Enumeration 
 Definition 
 Service enumeration is the process of identifying reachable services and learning enough about them to understand protocol, exposure, purpose, and likely risk. 
 Why it matters 
 Finding an open port is only the beginning. The security value comes from turning “something is listening” into “this is likely a public reverse proxy, a forgotten admin service, or a database that should not be reachable”.
This branch keeps service enumeration attack-surface-oriented: the networking note explains protocol identification mechanics, while this note stays focused on what the finding means for exposure and prioritization. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers enumerate services to:
- fingerprint protocols and versions
- find side doors and forgotten services
- prioritize targets for deeper testing
- detect drift between documented and actual surfaces 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- enumerate their own estate regularly
- validate service banners and exposure assumptions
- compare results against architecture and inventory
- feed unexpected findings into retirement and hardening work 
 Practical examples 
 
 a host exposes HTTPS and also an untracked SSH service 
 a public reverse proxy leaks alternate virtual hosts 
 a supposedly internal service responds externally on a high port

title: Subdomain Takeover
text:
Subdomain Takeover 
 Definition 
 Subdomain takeover is a class of exposure where a subdomain points to infrastructure or a service relationship that no longer exists, allowing an attacker to claim the underlying target and take control of the subdomain. 
 Why it matters 
 This is a classic example of attack surface drift. The problem is not just DNS configuration in theory; it is the operational gap between asset lifecycle and naming lifecycle.
The highest-value cases usually sit at the boundary between DNS ownership, third-party mappings, and deprovisioned infrastructure. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers search for:
- dangling CNAMEs
- decommissioned cloud app mappings
- service-provider claims that can be re-registered
- stale records left behind after vendor or environment changes 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- continuously review DNS ownership and target validity
- remove stale records promptly
- tie deprovisioning to DNS cleanup
- understand which third-party services are claimable after deletion 
 Practical examples 
 
 a subdomain still points to a deleted cloud app 
 a SaaS integration hostname remains live in DNS after offboarding 
 a bug bounty hunter claims a stale service mapping and serves content on the subdomain

  Public Asset Discovery

title: Third-Party Exposure
text:
Third-Party Exposure 
 Definition 
 Third-party exposure is the portion of your attack surface created by vendors, SaaS platforms, external APIs, CDNs, managed services, and other dependencies that handle traffic, content, authentication, or data on your behalf. 
 Why it matters 
 Modern attack surface extends beyond assets you host directly. DNS, auth providers, observability tools, CI/CD services, storage providers, and embedded integrations can all influence exposure and trust.
Keep the boundary tight: this note is about vendor-connected reachability and trust on exposed surfaces, not the entire software supply-chain problem. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- trusted vendor domains and callbacks
- stale SaaS mappings
- external APIs consumed unsafely
- hosted assets and widgets
- supply-chain or routing assumptions that widen reachability 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- model vendor-connected exposure explicitly
- review which third parties can serve content or influence requests
- remove stale integrations and callbacks
- treat outsourced functionality as part of the real surface 
 Practical examples 
 
 a deleted SaaS mapping leaves a claimable subdomain 
 an external API integration is trusted too broadly 
 a third-party file or widget origin widens exposure unexpectedly

title: Artifact Integrity
text:
Artifact Integrity 
 Definition 
 Artifact integrity is the assurance that build outputs, packages, images, and release artifacts have not been tampered with and can be traced back to the intended build process. 
 Why it matters 
 If you cannot trust what was built and shipped, security checks earlier in the pipeline lose much of their value. Integrity is about preserving trust from source to release.
This is distinct from  dependency-risk  and  sbom-and-provenance : the question here is whether the shipped artifact stayed trustworthy, not just what components it contains or where they came from. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers target artifact integrity by:
- tampering with build outputs
- swapping artifacts after CI
- abusing weak release controls
- exploiting gaps between build, storage, and deployment 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- limit who and what can write release artifacts
- trace artifacts back to specific builds and commits
- review storage and promotion paths
- separate build trust from deployment trust intentionally 
 Practical examples 
 
 a release artifact in storage is mutable after build 
 deployment pulls “latest” instead of a controlled immutable artifact 
 there is no reliable way to prove which source produced a shipped binary

  ci-cd-hardening  
  sbom-and-provenance  
  branch-protection-and-release-controls  
  Exposed Storage

title: ASVS as Dev Process Input
text:
ASVS as Dev Process Input 
 Definition 
 This note treats OWASP ASVS not as a post-hoc checklist, but as a development-process input for designing, reviewing, and verifying technical security controls during implementation. 
 Why it matters 
 DevSecOps needs requirements, not just scanners. ASVS gives a structured set of verification requirements that teams can use to shape architecture, backlog items, review criteria, and release readiness.
This note is about turning verification requirements into engineering inputs. It is narrower than  nist-ssdf  and less philosophical than  secure-by-design . 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers exploit the gap between “we run tools” and “we built the right controls”. If teams lack explicit verification requirements, vulnerabilities survive because nobody was concretely accountable for the control. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders can use ASVS to:
- define security expectations by control area
- connect requirements to implementation tasks
- improve review rigor
- avoid relying only on ad hoc tests or tooling 
 Practical examples 
 
 a team has SAST and dependency scans but no explicit verification requirement for access control or session handling 
 security review becomes more consistent once ASVS sections are mapped into stories and release gates

title: Branch Protection and Release Controls
text:
Branch Protection and Release Controls 
 Definition 
 Branch protection and release controls are the rules and governance mechanisms that determine who can change protected code paths, approve releases, and promote artifacts toward production. 
 Why it matters 
 A secure build pipeline can still be undermined if the code and release gates feeding it are weak. This topic connects code review, approval, separation of duties, and release discipline.
Keep it separate from  ci-cd-hardening : this note is about who can change or promote sensitive code paths, not the security posture of the automation environment itself. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- direct pushes to protected branches
- bypassable review gates
- weak release approvals
- overbroad maintainer rights
- manual shortcuts around formal release flow 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- protect sensitive branches and release tags
- require appropriate review and status checks
- restrict bypass powers
- define who can approve, build, and promote 
 Practical examples 
 
 admins can merge without checks and do so routinely 
 release tags are mutable or loosely controlled 
 production promotion relies on an undocumented manual shortcut

  artifact-integrity  
  secure-by-design

title: CI/CD Hardening
text:
CI/CD Hardening 
 Definition 
 CI/CD hardening is the practice of securing the build, test, and deployment pipeline so that automation becomes a trusted control path rather than an attack amplifier. 
 Why it matters 
 Pipelines often hold code access, secrets, signing material, release privileges, and deployment automation. A weak CI/CD system can become the shortest path from source compromise to production compromise.
This note stays focused on pipeline execution and build environment trust;  branch-protection-and-release-controls  covers governance around code and promotion, while  secrets-management  focuses on sensitive material itself. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers target CI/CD to:
- steal secrets
- run malicious steps
- tamper with artifacts
- abuse overly broad automation privileges
- pivot from build environment into infrastructure 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- lock down pipeline permissions
- isolate build environments
- minimize secret exposure in jobs
- review who can modify workflows and release logic
- treat pipeline definitions as sensitive code 
 Practical examples 
 
 any contributor can modify workflow logic on a sensitive branch 
 pipeline logs expose tokens or credentials 
 build runners have broad network reach and persistent state

title: Container Security
text:
Container Security 
 Definition 
 Container security is the practice of reducing risk in how containerized applications are built, configured, shipped, and run. 
 Why it matters 
 Containers make delivery easier, but they also package software, dependencies, configuration, and privilege assumptions into a highly portable unit. Weak base images, broad privileges, and poor defaults can scale insecure patterns quickly.
Container security is broader than  image-scanning : scanning is one useful control, but the full topic includes base image trust, privilege design, build context, runtime posture, and promotion discipline. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- overly privileged containers
- weak or bloated base images
- secrets baked into images
- exposed admin/debug tooling
- drift between what the image contains and what teams think it contains 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- reduce image complexity
- control privileges and capabilities carefully
- keep build context and image contents intentional
- separate build hygiene from runtime assumptions
- review how images are sourced and promoted 
 Practical examples 
 
 a container runs as root unnecessarily 
 debug tools and credentials are baked into production images 
 teams inherit a base image without understanding its maintenance state 
 
 Related notes

  supply-chain-security  
  artifact-integrity  
  Exposed Storage  
 
 References

  Foundational:  CISA Secure by Design — https://www.cisa.gov/resources-tools/resources/secure-by-design

title: Dependency Risk
text:
Dependency Risk 
 Definition 
 Dependency risk is the security risk introduced by direct and transitive third-party libraries, frameworks, packages, and their update and trust patterns. 
 Why it matters 
 Dependencies expand the codebase, privilege surface, and maintenance burden beyond what the product team directly writes. Risk comes not only from known CVEs, but from maintenance quality, version drift, trust decisions, and package ecosystem abuse.
Keep the boundary tight: this note is about upstream package trust and maintenance exposure, not artifact tampering or release traceability after the build. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- outdated packages with known issues
- poorly maintained libraries
- dependency confusion and typosquatting opportunities
- transitive packages nobody is watching 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- minimize dependency count where sensible
- track direct and transitive dependencies
- prioritize based on reachability and impact, not raw count alone
- establish update and retirement discipline 
 Practical examples 
 
 a vulnerable package lingers because nobody owns update cadence 
 a transitive dependency introduces risk the team did not know existed 
 package trust is assumed because “it is popular” 
 
 Related notes

  sbom-and-provenance

  image-scanning

title: Image Scanning
text:
Image Scanning 
 Definition 
 Image scanning is the process of inspecting container images for known vulnerabilities, risky packages, and other issues before promotion or deployment. 
 Why it matters 
 Image scanning is useful, but only when placed in the right context. It does not replace dependency management, build integrity, or secure base image strategy. It is one signal inside a larger DevSecOps system.
This note stays intentionally narrow: scanning helps surface known issues, but it cannot by itself prove a container is well designed, minimally privileged, or safely promoted. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers benefit when teams:
- trust scan output blindly
- ignore unscanned images and side paths
- use vulnerable bases because “the build passed”
- rely on scanning without fixing or prioritization 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- treat scanning as one control among many
- scan early and before release
- prioritize by exploitability and exposure, not just count
- understand scanner blind spots and false confidence risks 
 Practical examples 
 
 images pass a scan gate but still contain risky unused tooling and poor defaults 
 a side-loaded image path bypasses scanning entirely 
 teams scan but do not triage or remediate meaningfully

  dependency-risk  
  ci-cd-hardening

title: NIST SSDF
text:
NIST SSDF 
 Definition 
 The Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) is NIST’s framework for integrating software security into development practices across the organization, toolchain, and release process. 
 Why it matters 
 SSDF gives DevSecOps a durable structure. It turns “security in the pipeline” from a vague aspiration into concrete practices around preparing the organization, protecting software, producing well-secured software, and responding to vulnerabilities.
This note is the framework anchor for the branch;  secure-by-design  is the product philosophy, while  asvs-as-dev-process-input  is a concrete verification input teams can feed into delivery work. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers benefit when teams treat security as ad hoc tooling instead of a system. Weak environments, inconsistent checks, unclear roles, and informal release practices create openings long before runtime exploitation. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use SSDF to:
- define secure development practices
- establish secure environments and responsibilities
- integrate checks into the workflow
- improve response and remediation discipline 
 Practical examples 
 
 secure code review exists, but release approval and artifact integrity are informal 
 teams scan dependencies but lack a process for fixing or prioritizing findings 
 developers harden code but the build environment is weakly controlled

title: SBOM and Provenance
text:
SBOM and Provenance 
 Definition 
 An SBOM records what software components are in a product, while provenance provides traceability about how, where, and from what those artifacts were built. 
 Why it matters 
 You cannot govern software risk well if you do not know what is inside your artifacts or how they were produced. SBOM and provenance improve incident response, dependency review, release confidence, and downstream trust.
Keep the distinction sharp: SBOM and provenance improve visibility and traceability, while  artifact-integrity  focuses on whether artifacts stayed trustworthy and  dependency-risk  focuses on upstream package exposure. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers benefit when teams cannot answer:
- what components are in this release?
- which build produced it?
- what upstreams were involved?
- which versions are deployed where? 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- understand what is being shipped
- connect components to builds and releases
- improve response speed when new upstream risk appears
- use provenance to reinforce artifact trust 
 Practical examples 
 
 a team cannot quickly identify where a vulnerable transitive package is deployed 
 an artifact exists in production with unclear build origin 
 release records do not capture enough component detail for rapid triage

  artifact-integrity  
  dependency-risk

title: Secrets Management
text:
Secrets Management 
 Definition 
 Secrets management is the secure handling of credentials, tokens, keys, certificates, and other sensitive material across development, build, deployment, and runtime. 
 Why it matters 
 Secrets frequently bridge trust boundaries between source control, CI/CD, infrastructure, and production. Weak handling turns local convenience into systemic compromise.
This note is about the lifecycle and exposure of sensitive material itself, not the whole CI/CD environment or release-governance model around it. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- secrets in source control
- credentials in CI logs
- long-lived tokens in environment variables
- overprivileged secrets reused across systems
- stale secrets that were never rotated 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- minimize where secrets exist
- separate build-time and runtime secrets
- rotate and scope secrets intentionally
- reduce human handling of secrets
- review exposure in logs, artifacts, and config 
 Practical examples 
 
 a deploy token is printed in logs 
 the same long-lived credential is reused across environments 
 repo history still contains secrets that teams assume are “gone” 
 
 Related notes 
 
  ci-cd-hardening  
  supply-chain-security  
  artifact-integrity  
 
 References

title: Secure by Design
text:
Secure by Design 
 Definition 
 Secure by Design is the principle that software manufacturers and engineering teams should make security outcomes the default result of product design rather than shifting the burden to operators and end users. 
 Why it matters 
 This note matters because DevSecOps can become too tool-centric. Secure by Design reframes the goal: reduce exploitable conditions before deployment by changing defaults, product architecture, and engineering incentives.
Keep the boundary clear: this is a product and engineering philosophy, not the process framework of  nist-ssdf  or the verification catalog role of  asvs-as-dev-process-input . 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers benefit when products:
- ship insecure defaults
- require customers to harden everything manually
- expose dangerous functionality by default
- make risky configurations easy and safe ones hard 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use Secure by Design to:
- reduce exploitable exposure before runtime
- simplify secure defaults
- eliminate classes of mistakes at the product level
- shift security responsibility left onto the producer, not the customer alone 
 Practical examples 
 
 default admin interfaces remain enabled 
 public storage or debug features are exposed by default 
 product teams rely on docs telling customers to “configure securely later” 
 
 Related notes 
 
  nist-ssdf  
  branch-protection-and-release-controls  
  container-security  
  Exposed Storage  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  CISA Secure by Design — https://www.cisa.gov/resources-tools/resources/secure-by-design 
  Foundational:  CISA principles and approaches PDF — https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/2023-04/principles_approaches_for_security-by-design-default_508_0.pdf

title: Supply Chain Security
text:
Supply Chain Security 
 Definition 
 Supply chain security is the practice of reducing risk introduced by third-party code, build systems, dependencies, artifacts, signing paths, and release distribution processes. 
 Why it matters 
 Modern software is assembled from many upstream parts. DevSecOps is incomplete if it secures only first-party code while ignoring dependencies, transitive packages, build integrity, and release provenance.
This note is the umbrella for the supply-chain cluster:  dependency-risk  covers upstream package exposure,  artifact-integrity  covers tamper resistance in outputs, and  sbom-and-provenance  covers component and build traceability. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers target supply chains because one compromise can scale across many downstream consumers. Weaknesses in package trust, build systems, secrets, and artifacts can bypass strong runtime security entirely. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- understand where software components come from
- reduce unnecessary trust in upstreams
- secure the build and release path
- verify what is shipped, not just what is developed 
 Practical examples 
 
 a dependency update pulls in a malicious or compromised package 
 a build artifact is replaced after CI but before release 
 teams track vulnerabilities but not provenance or trust boundaries 
 
 Related notes 
 
  dependency-risk  
  artifact-integrity  
  sbom-and-provenance  
  Third-Party Exposure  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  OWASP Software Supply Chain Security Cheat Sheet — https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Software_Supply_Chain_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html 
  Foundational:  NIST SP 800-218 SSDF — https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/218/final

title: Caching and Security
text:
Caching and Security 
 Definition 
 Caching stores responses or computed results so they can be reused. This can happen in browsers, CDNs, reverse proxies, and application layers. 
 Why it matters 
 Caching is a performance feature, but it can become a security problem when:
- personalized content is cached incorrectly
- sensitive responses are stored or replayed
- cache keys do not vary on the right headers
- proxies and browsers interpret directives differently 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- sensitive data appearing in cached responses
- ways to poison cache entries
- differences between origin and cache behavior
- missing  Vary  or weak  Cache-Control  rules 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should understand:
- what can be cached safely
- which headers influence cache keys
- how auth and personalization interact with caching
- which layers cache traffic in the request path 
 Practical examples 
 
 A user-specific dashboard response is cached by a shared intermediary. 
 A CDN caches a response that should vary by authorization or cookie state. 
 A proxy caches a redirect or error page in a way that changes user behavior. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect cache-related headers 
curl   -I   https://example.com

 # Compare responses with different headers 
curl   -I   https://example.com   -H    "Authorization: Bearer test" 
curl   -I   https://example.com   -H    "Cookie: session=test" 
   

 Related notes 
 
  http-headers  
  reverse-proxies  
  cookies-and-sessions  
  load-balancers  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  MDN HTTP caching — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Caching 
  Foundational:  MDN Cache-Control — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Cache-Control 
  Testing / Lab:  PortSwigger Web Security Academy — https://portswigger.net/web-security

title: Client IP Trust
text:
Client IP Trust 
 Definition 
 Client IP trust is the question of how an application decides what the “real client IP” is when requests pass through proxies, load balancers, CDNs, or other intermediaries. 
 Why it matters 
 A surprising number of security controls depend on the client IP:
- rate limiting
- IP allowlists
- audit logs
- geolocation checks
- fraud controls
- admin access rules 
 If the app trusts the wrong header or trusts it too broadly, those controls can be bypassed or made unreliable. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test whether they can spoof IP-related headers such as:
-  X-Forwarded-For 
-  X-Real-IP 
- vendor-specific forwarding headers 
 If the backend trusts attacker-controlled values, logging, rate limiting, and access controls can be corrupted. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- define a single trusted proxy boundary
- only trust forwarding headers from that boundary
- understand framework-specific proxy trust settings
- verify how logs and controls derive the client IP 
 Practical examples 
 
 Express is configured to trust proxies too broadly, so a user can spoof  X-Forwarded-For . 
 An admin endpoint is restricted by IP, but the backend trusts an attacker-supplied header. 
 Audit logs store a spoofed IP rather than the actual source IP. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Test IP-related header behavior 
curl   -i   https://example.com   -H    "X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.4" 
curl   -i   https://example.com   -H    "X-Real-IP: 1.2.3.4" 
   

 Related notes 
 
  reverse-proxies  
  http-messages  
  API Rate Limiting  
  Test Client IP Spoofing  
  firewalls-and-network-boundaries  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  MDN X-Forwarded-For — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-Forwarded-For 
  Testing / Lab:  PortSwigger request smuggling academy — https://portswigger.net/web-security/request-smuggling

title: Cookies and Sessions
text:
Cookies and Sessions 
 Definition 
 HTTP is stateless by default, so applications use cookies and server-side or token-based session mechanisms to preserve user context across requests. A cookie is a header-based mechanism for storing state on the client side and sending it back to the server on later requests. 
 Why it matters 
 A large percentage of authentication and authorization bugs depend on misunderstanding cookies and sessions:
- insecure cookie attributes
- session fixation
- weak logout semantics
- CSRF exposure
- over-trusting stateless tokens
- confusing authentication with authorization 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- session identifiers in insecure contexts
- missing cookie protections such as  HttpOnly ,  Secure , and  SameSite 
- predictable or reusable sessions
- places where cookies are sent automatically by the browser 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should understand:
- when cookies are sent
- how attributes reduce risk
- what belongs in a server-side session vs token
- how session rotation and invalidation work
- how browser behavior changes security outcomes 
 Practical examples 
 
 A login cookie lacks  HttpOnly , making XSS impact worse. 
  SameSite=None  is used without  Secure , which is invalid and unsafe. 
 An app rotates nothing after login, so pre-auth and post-auth states share the same session identifier. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect Set-Cookie headers 
curl   -i   https://example.com/login

 # Persist cookies locally with curl 
curl   -c   cookies.txt   -b   cookies.txt   -i   https://example.com/profile
   

 Related notes 
 
  http-overview  
  http-messages  
  http-headers  
  tls-https  
  session-management  
  CSRF  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  MDN Set-Cookie header — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Set-Cookie 
  Foundational:  OWASP WSTG authentication and session testing — https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Se

title: DNS Resolution
text:
DNS Resolution 
 Definition 
 DNS resolution is the process of turning a hostname into the records needed to reach a destination, such as A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, or TXT records. It is the practical lookup path that connects naming to network reachability. 
 Why it matters 
 Security work often depends not just on DNS records existing, but on understanding how names resolve through chains of records and providers. Resolution affects:
- asset discovery
- ownership assumptions
- staging and forgotten environments
- third-party dependencies
- takeover risk through dangling CNAMEs 
 This note focuses on how names resolve in practice.  dns-security  covers DNS as an attack-surface and ownership problem, while  subdomain-takeover  covers one concrete exploit path that falls out of bad DNS hygiene. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use DNS resolution to:
- discover how a hostname is actually mapped
- identify external providers in the resolution chain
- detect stale CNAMEs pointing to removed infrastructure
- infer relationships between apps, environments, and vendors 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should understand:
- how authoritative answers are produced
- which names depend on third parties
- whether CNAME chains point to decommissioned resources
- which records expose unnecessary information about the environment 
 Practical examples 
 
  app.example.com  resolves through a CNAME to a cloud service that no longer exists. 
 Internal naming patterns become obvious from public DNS records. 
 A CDN or load balancer sits in the resolution path, masking the origin architecture. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Trace record resolution 
dig   app.example.com
dig   app.example.com   CNAME
dig   +short   app.example.com

 # Inspect full answer sections 
dig   app.example.com   ANY
   

 Related notes 
 
  dns-security  
  subdomain-takeover  
  attack-surface-mapping  
  load-balancers  
  service-enumeration  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  Cloudflare Learning Center on DNS re

title: DNS Security
text:
DNS Security 
 Definition 
 DNS translates names into network destinations. In practice, it is the naming layer that lets browsers, clients, and tooling find the IPs and services behind domains and subdomains. 
 Why it matters 
 A lot of security work depends on DNS:
- domain and subdomain inventory
- stale records
- cloud service mappings
- subdomain or DNS takeover risk
- mail and identity-related configuration
- visibility into external attack surface 
 If your DNS is messy, your attack surface is usually messier than your diagrams suggest. 
 This note is about DNS as security exposure and ownership hygiene.  dns-resolution  is the lookup-path note, and  subdomain-takeover  is the specific exploitation note. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use DNS to:
- enumerate subdomains
- identify old or dangling records
- discover third-party dependencies
- find misconfigured records that could lead to takeover or unexpected ownership 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- maintain an accurate inventory of domains and subdomains
- remove stale mappings
- understand what external providers are authoritative for which names
- review changes as part of attack surface management 
 Practical examples 
 
 A subdomain still points to a decommissioned cloud resource. 
 A staging hostname remains in DNS long after the environment was removed. 
 DNS records reveal internal naming schemes or forgotten services. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Basic lookups 
dig   example.com
dig   sub.example.com   CNAME

 # Quick summary lookup 
nslookup   sub.example.com
   

 Related notes 
 
  dns-resolution  
  stale-dns-records  
  Subdomain Takeover  
  attack-surface-mapping  
 
 References

title: Firewalls and Network Boundaries
text:
Firewalls and Network Boundaries 
 Definition 
 Firewalls and network boundaries control which traffic is allowed between networks, hosts, and services. In cloud environments, this often includes security groups, network ACLs, private subnets, and load balancer exposure rules. 
 Why it matters 
 A service can be perfectly written and still be dangerously exposed. Security depends on boundaries:
- what is public
- what is internal
- who can initiate connections
- which services can talk to which other services 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use boundary mistakes to:
- reach admin services that should have been private
- pivot from one service into another
- determine whether segmentation is real or only assumed
- use SSRF or internal footholds to access private resources 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should continuously validate:
- that public-facing services are only the intended ones
- that databases, caches, and admin panels are not Internet-reachable
- that internal services are not over-permissive
- that architecture diagrams match actual exposure 
 Practical examples 
 
 PostgreSQL is reachable from the Internet because a security group is too broad. 
 An internal admin service is protected only by obscurity, not by a boundary. 
 A reverse proxy is public, but the backend is also accidentally public on a different port. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # External perspective 
nmap   -sV   your-host.example.com

 # Local perspective 
lsof   -i   -P   -n
   

 Related notes 
 
  ports-and-services  
  nat-and-private-networks  
  attack-surface-mapping  
  nmap-scanning  
 
 References

title: HTTP Headers
text:
HTTP Headers 
 Definition 
 HTTP headers are metadata fields attached to requests and responses. They shape how clients, proxies, caches, and servers interpret traffic. 
 Why it matters 
 Headers are one of the main places where security-relevant behavior is controlled:
- cookies
- caching
- CORS
- forwarding
- content types
- content security policy
- authentication
- transport security 
 A lot of attacks are really header interpretation problems. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers inspect and manipulate headers to:
- discover hidden behavior
- bypass assumptions about origin or client identity
- trigger parser differences across components
- abuse weak CORS or caching controls
- learn how auth and sessions are carried 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should understand:
- which headers are set intentionally
- which ones are added by proxies or frameworks
- which security headers are missing
- which headers are trusted and why
- how the same header may be interpreted differently by different layers 
 Practical examples 
 
 A response lacks  Content-Security-Policy , increasing XSS impact. 
  Cache-Control  is wrong on sensitive responses. 
  X-Forwarded-For  is trusted without a strong proxy boundary. 
  Authorization  behaves differently depending on the proxy path. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect response headers 
curl   -I   https://example.com

 # Send custom request headers 
curl   -i   https://example.com   -H    "X-Test: 1" 
   

 Related notes 
 
  http-overview  
  http-messages  
  client-ip-trust  
  caching-and-security  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  MDN HTTP headers — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers 
  Foundational:  OWASP Cheat Sheet Series — https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/ 
  Testing / Lab:  PortSwigger Web Security Academy — https://portswigger.net/web-security

title: HTTP Messages
text:
HTTP Messages 
 Definition 
 HTTP messages are the raw units exchanged between client and server. Requests include a request line, headers, and an optional body. Responses include a status line, headers, and an optional body. 
 Why it matters 
 Many web vulnerabilities become obvious only when you look at the actual message:
- which headers are present
- how cookies are sent
- whether a body is forwarded
- how content length is interpreted
- whether proxies and backends parse the same message the same way 
 Understanding message structure is one of the best transitions from “web developer” to “security-minded engineer”. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers study messages to:
- tamper with headers
- replay or modify requests
- probe parser differences
- inspect hidden behavior such as debug flags or trust headers
- understand how auth and sessions are carried 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use message-level thinking to:
- review proxy behavior
- validate safe header handling
- inspect session and cookie flows
- understand why a request was accepted or rejected
- debug request smuggling and desync-style issues 
 Practical examples 
 
  X-Forwarded-For  is trusted by the backend but can be spoofed when the proxy boundary is weak. 
  Content-Length  and  Transfer-Encoding  are interpreted differently by different components. 
 The same request looks harmless in app code but dangerous in the raw wire format. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Show raw request/response behavior as much as curl exposes 
curl   -v   https://example.com

 # Send a crafted body 
curl   -v   -X   POST   https://example.com/login   -H    "Content-Type: application/json"    -d    '{"user":"a"}' 
   

 Related notes 
 
  http-overview  
  reverse-proxies  
  packet-analysis  
  wireshark-workflows  
  Request Smuggling  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  MDN HTTP messages — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/Messages

title: HTTP Overview
text:
HTTP Overview 
 Definition 
 HTTP is the application-layer protocol used to transfer web resources. It follows a request/response model in which a client sends a request and a server returns a response. In practice, HTTP is the foundation for websites, APIs, browser sessions, and many backend integrations. 
 Why it matters 
 A huge amount of security work sits directly on top of HTTP:
- authentication flows
- cookies and sessions
- reverse proxies
- headers and caching
- CORS
- request smuggling
- API behavior 
 If you do not understand how HTTP actually works, many vulnerabilities look like magic instead of predictable protocol abuse. 
 This note stays at the protocol-model level.  http-messages  covers the raw request and response shape, while  http-headers  focuses on the fields that actually steer security behavior. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers care about:
- request methods and semantics
- headers that change behavior
- proxy chains
- response codes
- cookies and auth tokens
- the exact shape of requests and responses 
 Modern web attacks often come from tiny differences in HTTP interpretation between browser, proxy, CDN, and backend. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders need HTTP fluency to:
- set correct security headers
- understand trust boundaries at proxies
- design safer cookie and auth handling
- debug misconfigurations instead of guessing
- reason about CORS and browser-only controls 
 Practical examples 
 
 A browser sends cookies automatically on some cross-site requests. 
 A reverse proxy injects or rewrites headers before the request hits your backend. 
 A  GET  endpoint that changes state makes CSRF and caching behavior more dangerous. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect raw headers 
curl   -i   https://example.com

 # Send a custom header 
curl   -i   -H    "X-Debug: 1"    https://example.com
   

 Related notes 
 
  http-messages  
  http-headers  
  cookies-and-sessions  
  reverse-proxies  
  packet-analysis

title: Load Balancers
text:
Load Balancers 
 Definition 
 A load balancer distributes traffic across backend services and often acts as a critical entry point for TLS termination, health checks, routing, and traffic normalization. 
 Why it matters 
 In modern systems, load balancers are often part of the security boundary whether you planned for that or not. They influence:
- client IP forwarding
- TLS termination
- path and host routing
- health-check exposure
- header normalization
- which services are reachable and how 
 This note is about entrypoint routing, exposure, and operational trust at the front door.  reverse-proxies  goes deeper on HTTP normalization and parser disagreement. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look at load balancers to understand:
- how requests are routed
- whether direct backend access also exists
- what headers are injected
- whether health or admin paths are accidentally public
- where the real trust boundary begins 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should understand:
- what the load balancer terminates or rewrites
- whether backends are directly reachable
- how client IP is propagated
- which paths or hosts are routed to which services
- what health checks or diagnostics are externally visible 
 Practical examples 
 
 TLS terminates at the load balancer, but the backend trusts plaintext internal traffic too much. 
 A health endpoint reveals internal state because it is publicly reachable through the load balancer. 
 The origin server is exposed directly as well as through the load balancer, bypassing some expected controls. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect entrypoint behavior 
curl   -I   https://example.com

 # Compare host/path routing where appropriate 
curl   -I   https://example.com/health
curl   -I   -H    "Host: alt.example.com"    https://example.com
   

 Related notes 
 
  tls-https  
  reverse-proxies  
  client-ip-trust  
  firewalls-and-network-boundaries  
 
 References

title: Metadata Endpoints
text:
Metadata Endpoints 
 Definition 
 Metadata endpoints are special internal service endpoints, common in cloud environments, that provide information or credentials about the running instance, environment, or attached identity. 
 Why it matters 
 Metadata endpoints matter because they are usually not intended for direct public use, but they may still be reachable by applications running inside the environment. That makes them a critical SSRF and internal reachability topic. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers care about metadata endpoints because:
- an SSRF bug may let them query instance metadata
- metadata services can expose credentials, tokens, or environment details
- internal-only assumptions are often wrong once server-side request capability exists 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should understand:
- whether metadata endpoints are reachable from workloads
- what protections the cloud platform provides
- how SSRF transforms metadata access into credential exposure
- how to reduce unnecessary access from applications 
 Practical examples 
 
 A server-side fetch feature can reach cloud metadata and leak temporary credentials. 
 A containerized workload can access metadata because network controls are too broad. 
 A team assumes “not public” means “safe”, ignoring server-side request paths. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Example placeholder for controlled internal testing only 
curl   http://169.254.169.254/
   

 Related notes 
 
  nat-and-private-networks  
  firewalls-and-network-boundaries  
  load-balancers  
  SSRF  
 
 References

title: NAT and Private Networks
text:
NAT and Private Networks 
 Definition 
 Private networks use non-public IP ranges internally, while NAT (Network Address Translation) lets those hosts communicate outward or appear behind a smaller set of public addresses. This creates a separation between public reachability and internal addressing. 
 Why it matters 
 This note matters because many modern attacks depend on the difference between:
- what is reachable from the Internet
- what is reachable from inside the application environment 
 This is why SSRF, cloud metadata access, and lateral movement can be much more serious than they first appear. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for ways to make a public-facing system talk to private addresses on their behalf. That is one reason SSRF is dangerous: the app may be able to access internal hosts or metadata endpoints even when the attacker cannot directly do so. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders need to understand:
- which services are internal-only
- which egress and internal routing paths exist
- how applications can reach metadata, admin APIs, or databases
- how NAT and private subnets can create a false sense of safety if app-level bugs still allow internal access 
 Practical examples 
 
 A server-side image fetcher can request  http://169.254.169.254  in some environments. 
 A backend in a private subnet can still access internal APIs that external users cannot see. 
 A database is not public, but a compromised app server can reach it directly. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect local routes / interfaces 
ip   route
ip   addr

 # Test internal reachability from a controlled host 
nc   -vz    10 .0.0.5    8080 
   

 Related notes 
 
  tcp-ip-basics  
  firewalls-and-network-boundaries  
  dns-security  
  ssrf  
 
 References

title: Nmap Scanning
text:
Nmap Scanning 
 Definition 
 Nmap is a network discovery and service enumeration tool widely used for identifying live hosts, open ports, protocol details, and service versions. 
 Why it matters 
 Nmap is one of the fastest ways to turn vague assumptions into concrete evidence:
- what is open?
- what is closed?
- what is filtered?
- what software appears to be listening?
- does the real surface match the intended architecture? 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use Nmap to:
- identify reachable hosts
- fingerprint services
- detect forgotten endpoints
- prioritize likely attack paths 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use Nmap against their own environments to:
- validate exposure
- catch unexpected listening services
- detect drift between diagrams and reality
- confirm segmentation assumptions 
 Practical examples 
 
 Running  nmap -sV  against a VPS reveals a service you forgot was still deployed. 
 Service detection identifies a development dashboard exposed over HTTPS. 
 A host expected to expose only 443 also exposes SSH and a framework dev port. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Service/version detection 
nmap   -sV   your-host.example.com

 # Faster top-port scan 
nmap   --top-ports    1000    your-host.example.com

 # More detailed detection (use carefully and legally) 
nmap   -A   your-host.example.com

title: Packet Analysis
text:
Packet Analysis 
 Definition 
 Packet analysis is the practice of inspecting individual network packets or protocol conversations to understand what systems are actually sending, receiving, and interpreting. 
 Why it matters 
 Packet analysis is where many fuzzy security ideas become concrete:
- what exactly went over the wire?
- which headers were really present?
- was DNS resolved before the request?
- did the app retry, redirect, or downgrade?
- did the network path behave differently than the code suggested? 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use packet analysis to:
- confirm exploit assumptions
- understand protocol edge cases
- see whether injected values survive intermediaries
- map traffic patterns for reconnaissance or debugging 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders use packet analysis to:
- validate network boundaries
- troubleshoot authentication or session issues
- inspect proxy behavior
- understand how requests traverse the environment 
 Practical examples 
 
 You capture login traffic and verify the  Set-Cookie  header and redirect chain. 
 You inspect whether a reverse proxy normalized headers before the backend received them. 
 You compare failed and successful requests to see which network-level details differ. 
 
 Suggested workflow 
 
 Define the traffic you care about. 
 Capture only the relevant interface and time window. 
 Filter aggressively. 
 Compare expected behavior with observed behavior. 
 Convert findings into architecture or security notes.

  wireshark-workflows  
  http-messages  
  cookies-and-sessions  
  reverse-proxies  
 
 References

title: Ports and Services
text:
Ports and Services 
 Definition 
 A port is a logical endpoint on a host used to identify a specific network service. A service is the application or daemon listening on that port, such as SSH on 22 or HTTPS on 443. 
 Why it matters 
 An open port is not automatically a vulnerability, but it is always part of the attack surface. Security starts with understanding which services are exposed, why they are exposed, and whether that exposure is intentional. 
 This note is about exposed listening surface.  service-enumeration  starts once you want to learn what that exposed service actually is and how it behaves. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use ports and services to:
- find entry points
- identify protocols and software versions
- detect forgotten admin interfaces
- distinguish public surface from private surface 
 Even before exploiting application logic, an attacker asks: what is listening, what should not be listening, and what can I learn from banners, timing, and protocol behavior? 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should treat every listening service as something that needs justification:
- why is it exposed?
- who needs access?
- should it bind to localhost only?
- should it be behind a reverse proxy or VPN?
- is version detection leaking useful metadata? 
 Practical examples 
 
 PostgreSQL on  5432  is accidentally reachable from the Internet instead of being limited to internal traffic. 
 Redis is exposed without authentication because it was intended only for local development. 
 An old admin dashboard is still listening on a high-numbered port and never made it into the architecture docs. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Scan local or remote services 
nmap   -sV    127 .0.0.1
nmap   -sV   your-host.example.com

 # Test one service manually 
nc   -vz   your-host.example.com    443

title: Reverse Proxies
text:
Reverse Proxies 
 Definition 
 A reverse proxy sits in front of one or more backend services and forwards requests to them. Examples include Nginx, Envoy, HAProxy, cloud load balancers, and CDNs acting as HTTP front doors. 
 Why it matters 
 Reverse proxies are one of the most important trust boundaries in modern systems. They affect:
- header forwarding
- client IP handling
- TLS termination
- path rewriting
- caching
- request normalization
- which component interprets the HTTP message first 
 A lot of serious vulnerabilities happen because the proxy and the backend do not agree on what the request means. 
 This note focuses on HTTP interpretation and trust translation at the edge.  load-balancers  covers traffic distribution and entrypoint routing more broadly. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test proxies to find:
- parser differences
- trust-header abuse
- request smuggling opportunities
- admin paths reachable only through weird routing behavior
- cache poisoning and normalization edge cases 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders need to know:
- which headers are injected or trusted
- how forwarded client identity is determined
- whether the backend expects sanitized requests
- where TLS ends
- whether the proxy and backend parse ambiguous requests consistently 
 Practical examples 
 
 The backend trusts  X-Forwarded-For  from any source. 
 A proxy strips one header but the backend still uses a different equivalent header. 
 Different interpretations of  Content-Length  and  Transfer-Encoding  create desync issues. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect proxy-added headers 
curl   -v   https://example.com

 # Compare direct backend vs proxy behavior when available 
curl   -v   https://proxy.example.com/health
   

 Related notes 
 
  http-messages  
  client-ip-trust  
  load-balancers  
  firewalls-and-network-boundaries  
  request-smuggling  
 
 References
