title: Service Enumeration
text:
Service Enumeration 
 Definition 
 Service enumeration is the process of identifying which services are running on a host and extracting useful details such as protocol, software family, version clues, banners, and reachable paths. 
 Why it matters 
 Enumeration turns “something is open” into “this is likely Nginx in front of an app, and SSH is exposed too”. That is the difference between surface visibility and actionable understanding. 
 This note begins after initial exposure is known.  ports-and-services  covers the existence of reachable listeners; service enumeration covers extracting enough detail to reason about risk. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use service enumeration to:
- fingerprint software
- identify exposed admin services
- prioritize attack paths
- correlate services with known behaviors or misconfigurations
- distinguish production traffic paths from forgotten side doors 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should enumerate their own assets to:
- validate intended exposure
- catch drift and shadow services
- review banner leakage
- understand whether the environment matches its docs and diagrams 
 Practical examples 
 
 Port 443 is open, but enumeration reveals a developer dashboard under the same host. 
 SSH is exposed publicly on a box that was assumed to be reachable only via bastion. 
 A service banner leaks framework or version details you did not intend to disclose. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Version/service detection 
nmap   -sV   example.com

 # More aggressive detection 
nmap   -A   example.com

 # Grab banners manually where useful 
nc   -vz   example.com    22 
curl   -I   https://example.com

title: Subdomain Takeover
text:
Subdomain Takeover 
 Definition 
 Subdomain takeover happens when a DNS record still points to an external service that is no longer owned or provisioned by the intended organization, allowing an attacker to claim that target and serve content under the abandoned subdomain. 
 Why It Matters 
 It turns stale DNS into real attack surface. A forgotten record can become a trusted-looking host for phishing, session theft, script hosting, cookie scope abuse, or internal trust bypass assumptions. 
 Attacker Perspective 
 Attackers search for dangling CNAMEs, deprovisioned SaaS targets, expired cloud app mappings, and provider-specific error pages that indicate a hostname can still be claimed. 
 Defender Perspective 
 Defenders should treat DNS ownership as part of asset lifecycle management. Every public hostname needs an owner, a live target, and a removal path when the backing service is retired. 
 Practical Examples 
 
  blog.example.com  still points to a deleted GitHub Pages or Azure resource that an attacker can re-register. 
 A staging hostname remains mapped to a decommissioned SaaS app and becomes attacker-controlled. 
 A takeover-ready hostname is later referenced by employees, documentation, or automation because the DNS name still looks legitimate. 
 
 Related Notes

  DNS Resolution  
  Attack Surface Mapping  
  Subdomain Enumeration

title: TCP/IP Basics
text:
TCP/IP Basics 
 Definition 
 TCP/IP is the practical foundation of network communication on the Internet. IP provides addressing and routing between hosts, while TCP provides reliable transport with sequencing, retransmission, and connection management. 
 Why it matters 
 Most security problems are not “just code problems”. They also depend on how systems become reachable, how traffic is routed, and what assumptions are made across layers. If you do not understand how IP and TCP fit together, you will struggle to reason about exposure, packet capture, SSRF reachability, or service enumeration. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers care about TCP/IP because it determines:
- whether a target is reachable
- which ports can be probed
- how connections are established and torn down
- what can be fingerprinted from network behavior 
 A lot of offensive work starts before any application exploit: discover a host, identify an exposed service, understand how the traffic flows, then move upward into HTTP, APIs, authentication, or business logic. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders use TCP/IP understanding to:
- segment systems properly
- reduce unnecessary exposure
- validate security group and firewall behavior
- understand packet captures and connection logs
- separate “application bug” from “network path” issues 
 Practical examples 
 
 Your Node API is listening on  0.0.0.0:3000  instead of  127.0.0.1:3000 , so it is reachable outside the host. 
 A reverse proxy forwards traffic to an internal backend over TCP, and the backend trusts headers that came from the proxy path. 
 An SSRF bug becomes more dangerous when the app can open TCP connections to private IPs. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Show listening TCP/UDP sockets 
lsof   -i   -P   -n

 # Check routes and interfaces (macOS/Linux variants differ) 
netstat   -rn
ip   addr
ip   route

 # Test TCP reachability 
nc   -vz    127 .0.0.1    3000 
   

 Related notes 
 
  ports-and-services  
  http-overview  
  nat-and-private

title: TLS and HTTPS
text:
TLS and HTTPS 
 Definition 
 HTTPS is HTTP carried over TLS. TLS provides confidentiality, integrity, and server authentication for traffic that would otherwise be sent in cleartext over the network. 
 Why it matters 
 A lot of developers treat HTTPS as a checkbox, but from a security perspective it changes:
- what can be observed in transit
- how certificates establish trust
- whether cookies can safely use the  Secure  attribute
- how reverse proxies terminate and forward traffic
- whether client and server identity assumptions are valid 
 Without a practical understanding of TLS, it is easy to misread exposure and overestimate the safety of “encrypted traffic”. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers care about TLS because:
- weak or broken trust chains can enable interception
- mixed-content or downgrade issues can weaken protections
- proxy termination points may create internal trust confusion
- certificate mismatches can reveal infrastructure mistakes
- HTTPS does not protect against server-side bugs, only traffic exposure 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should understand:
- where TLS terminates
- which certificates are in use
- whether internal hops remain encrypted
- how HSTS reduces downgrade risk
- how cookies and auth should behave differently under HTTPS 
 Practical examples 
 
 A login flow sets cookies but forgets  Secure , weakening transport guarantees. 
 TLS terminates at a load balancer, but traffic to the backend is sent internally over plaintext. 
 A staging hostname has a certificate mismatch that reveals infrastructure drift. 
 
 Useful commands 
      # Inspect a TLS endpoint 
openssl   s_client   -connect   example.com:443   -servername   example.com

 # Show HTTP response headers, including HSTS if present 
curl   -I   https://example.com
   

 Related notes

  reverse-proxies  
  cookies-and-sessions  
  load-balancers

title: Wireshark Workflows
text:
Wireshark Workflows 
 Definition 
 Wireshark is a packet capture and protocol analysis tool used to inspect network traffic at a much lower level than browser devtools or application logs. 
 Why it matters 
 Logs and framework abstractions often hide the exact traffic behavior you need to understand. Wireshark lets you observe:
- protocol conversations
- retransmissions
- DNS lookups
- HTTP request/response patterns
- TLS handshakes
- filtering by host, port, or protocol 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use packet-level visibility to:
- understand how protocols behave in practice
- observe what metadata leaks
- see timing and retransmission clues
- confirm assumptions about traffic patterns 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders use Wireshark to:
- troubleshoot protocol issues
- validate whether sensitive data is exposed in transit
- understand DNS and HTTP flows
- build intuition that improves appsec and infrastructure debugging 
 Practical examples 
 
 Capture traffic while logging into your app and inspect DNS, TLS, and HTTP stages. 
 Compare browser behavior with  curl  behavior. 
 Confirm whether a service is actually speaking the protocol you think it is. 
 
 Useful filters 
     http
dns
tcp.port == 443
ip.addr == 10.0.0.5

  dns-security  
  tcp-ip-basics

title: Active Recon
text:
Active Recon 
 Definition 
 Active recon is information gathering that directly interacts with the target’s infrastructure, services, or applications to validate or extend what passive recon discovered. 
 Why it matters 
 Passive recon finds possibilities. Active recon turns them into evidence. It is where reachable hosts, services, routes, and trust assumptions get validated.
That makes it operationally later than  passive-recon  and narrower than general  enumeration , which organizes multiple discovery streams into an actionable target picture. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use active recon to:
- probe live hosts and ports
- verify services and versions
- test route and endpoint availability
- separate stale leads from live exposure 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- perform active recon against their own assets regularly
- validate internet-facing services intentionally
- compare live results against inventory and architectural expectations 
 Practical examples 
 
 a guessed subdomain is confirmed live 
 host discovery finds a service not recorded in inventory 
 probing reveals alternate virtual hosts or legacy routes

title: Company Mapping
text:
Company Mapping 
 Definition 
 Company mapping is the process of connecting domains, brands, subsidiaries, vendors, public identities, and infrastructure clues into a coherent model of what the target actually operates. 
 Why it matters 
 Many targets do not present themselves as one clean namespace. Brands, subsidiaries, vendor relationships, and historical naming make the real surface larger and more fragmented than the official homepage suggests.
This note is about ownership and organizational shape, not raw asset collection or service validation. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use company mapping to:
- find assets outside the obvious domain
- connect vendors and hosted services
- identify likely environment naming schemes
- expand target visibility without guessing blindly 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- understand how outsiders can infer their estate
- know which brands and domains imply ownership
- clean up stale or misleading external signals 
 Practical examples 
 
 multiple product domains map back to the same company 
 support portals and hosted login pages reveal vendor relationships 
 an acquired brand still points to reachable legacy infrastructure 
 
 Related notes 
 
  public-asset-discovery  
  Third-Party Exposure  
  passive-recon

title: Enumeration
text:
Enumeration 
 Definition 
 Enumeration is the focused, methodical expansion of discovered leads into concrete, validated knowledge about reachable services, routes, identities, and behaviors. 
 Why it matters 
 Recon finds candidate assets. Enumeration turns those candidates into actionable understanding: what is live, what responds, what the surface likely contains, and what merits deeper testing.
Operationally, this sits between broad recon and deeper validation:  subdomain-enumeration  and  host-and-port-discovery  expand the target set, while  service-validation  helps confirm which findings deserve testing time. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers enumerate to:
- confirm exposure
- refine target lists
- identify patterns across hosts and apps
- discover low-friction paths into higher-value testing 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- treat enumeration as a mirror for what outsiders can learn quickly
- use it to validate ownership, inventory, and exposure assumptions
- improve how findings are handed into remediation and governance 
 Practical examples 
 
 enumerating subdomains reveals which ones are live and what they host 
 route and service validation identifies which assets deserve manual testing next 
 enumeration finds a mismatch between what is documented and what actually responds

  subdomain-enumeration

title: Host and Port Discovery
text:
Host and Port Discovery 
 Definition 
 Host and port discovery is the process of finding live hosts and the reachable ports and services they expose. 
 Why it matters 
 Once domains and candidates are known, host and port discovery validates what is actually listening and reachable. This is one of the cleanest ways to separate stale surface from live surface.
Keep the boundary clear: this note is about reachability and exposed ports, while  service-validation  and networking  Service Enumeration  go deeper into what those responses actually mean. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use host and port discovery to:
- find reachable services
- detect alternate entry points
- prioritize hosts for deeper enumeration
- compare service exposure across environments 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- scan their own public assets regularly
- validate expected exposure
- detect accidental internet-facing services
- compare scan results to inventory and intended design 
 Practical examples 
 
 scanning reveals SSH or database ports that should not be public 
 one host exposes more services than its peers 
 a public service responds on a nonstandard port and was never documented

title: Passive Recon
text:
Passive Recon 
 Definition 
 Passive recon is information gathering that does not directly interact with the target’s systems in a way that would normally generate target-side traffic. 
 Why it matters 
 Passive recon is low-friction, broad, and often enough to reveal large parts of the external attack surface before touching the target at all.
Keep the boundary clear: passive recon is about collecting clues without direct target interaction, not proving those clues are live the way  active-recon  does. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use passive recon to collect:
- domain and subdomain clues
- historical records
- public certificates
- exposed documents and metadata
- public employee, vendor, and technology signals 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- understand what public information leaks about their estate
- reduce unnecessary metadata and stale artifacts
- review public exposure from a third-party observer perspective 
 Practical examples 
 
 certificate logs reveal subdomains 
 archived URLs show routes no longer linked from the UI 
 public docs expose internal naming patterns 
 
 Related notes

  public-asset-discovery  
  company-mapping  
  subdomain-enumeration

title: Public Asset Discovery
text:
Public Asset Discovery 
 Definition 
 Public asset discovery is the process of identifying internet-facing domains, subdomains, hosts, services, APIs, storage surfaces, and supporting infrastructure that belong to or materially relate to a target. 
 Why it matters 
 Before testing anything deeply, you need to know what exists. Public asset discovery is one of the fastest ways to reveal undocumented, legacy, or weakly governed exposure.
This note stays focused on finding public-facing assets themselves, not proving ownership relationships like  company-mapping  or inferring implementation details like  tech-stack-fingerprinting . 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- domains and subdomains
- cloud-hosted entry points
- public APIs
- storage buckets and artifact paths
- externally reachable admin or support surfaces 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- maintain visibility into internet-facing assets
- identify ownership gaps
- review which assets remain reachable but unsupported
- feed discovery results into attack surface mapping and retirement 
 Practical examples 
 
 a forgotten static site still serves content 
 a public API host is reachable but absent from formal docs 
 public assets spread across multiple vendors and providers 
 
 Related notes

  subdomain-enumeration  
  host-and-port-discovery

title: Recon to Testing Handoff
text:
Recon to Testing Handoff 
 Definition 
 Recon-to-testing handoff is the transition from discovery and validation into focused security testing, with enough context to choose the right next activity instead of probing blindly. 
 Why it matters 
 Recon without handoff becomes a pile of findings. The real value comes when discovery narrows into clear next steps: access control testing, API review, SSRF assessment, admin interface review, or version drift analysis.
This note is where discovery becomes testing strategy: the handoff should preserve enough context to decide between web, API, and infrastructure-flavored next steps instead of treating everything as generic scanning. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use handoff to:
- prioritize the highest-value assets
- choose the right test class for each target
- turn discovery into reproducible workflows
- avoid noisy, low-signal testing 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- structure findings so remediation teams can act
- connect exposure findings to risk and ownership
- make attack surface review part of the testing workflow, not a separate dead-end exercise 
 Practical examples 
 
 endpoint discovery leads into API inventory and BOLA testing 
 a found admin interface leads into access-control and auth testing 
 an exposed metadata-capable internal path leads into SSRF review

title: Recon
text:
Recon 
 Definition 
 Reconnaissance is the structured process of gathering information about a target in order to understand its assets, exposures, technologies, and likely avenues for deeper testing. 
 Why it matters 
 Good recon raises the quality of every downstream activity. It improves attack surface mapping, reduces wasted testing, exposes undocumented assets, and helps distinguish real opportunities from noise.
This note is the umbrella for the branch:  passive-recon  covers low-interaction discovery, while  active-recon  covers direct validation against live targets. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers use recon to:
- discover assets not visible in the main product UI
- find weakly governed or forgotten systems
- connect naming, hosting, and technology clues
- prioritize the next stage of testing 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use recon thinking to:
- see what outsiders can actually learn
- compare observed assets with owned inventory
- identify exposure drift and stale systems
- improve retirement and governance discipline 
 Practical examples 
 
 a recon pass finds a staging host and an old API version the team forgot 
 DNS and ASN data reveal more public assets than product docs mention 
 JavaScript and mobile artifacts expose hidden routes 
 
 Related notes 
 
  passive-recon  
  active-recon

title: Scope Validation
text:
Scope Validation 
 Definition 
 Scope validation is the process of confirming which discovered assets are actually in-scope, owned by the target, and appropriate for further testing. 
 Why it matters 
 Recon produces ambiguity. Without scope validation, it is easy to waste time, test the wrong target, or confuse vendor-managed infrastructure with the organization’s owned surface.
This note should stay narrow: it is about ownership and in-scope clarity, not broad discovery or technical validation of the service itself. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers and bug bounty hunters validate scope to:
- avoid dead ends
- avoid off-limits assets
- focus effort on relevant, testable systems
- distinguish owned from adjacent infrastructure 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- make ownership and scope boundaries clearer
- reduce ambiguous naming and stale public signals
- understand how easy it is for outsiders to misclassify assets 
 Practical examples 
 
 a subdomain belongs to a third-party SaaS instance rather than the target 
 a public IP resolves to a shared platform and needs ownership confirmation 
 an acquired brand’s domain is still live but not part of the current testing scope

  Third-Party Exposure  
  External Attack Surface

title: Service Validation
text:
Service Validation 
 Definition 
 Service validation is the process of confirming what an observed service actually is, how it behaves, and whether it is relevant enough to move into deeper testing. 
 Why it matters 
 Not every open port or responding route matters equally. Validation helps distinguish noise, dead ends, and edge infrastructure from meaningful application or control-plane targets.
This is later than  host-and-port-discovery  in the workflow: first find what answers, then determine what it really is and whether it should transition into testing. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers validate services to:
- confirm protocol and role
- understand whether the service is worth deeper analysis
- identify alternate paths into more sensitive functionality
- avoid spending time on low-value or misleading leads 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- validate observed services against intended roles
- understand what each public service actually exposes
- feed discrepancies into inventory and hardening work 
 Practical examples 
 
 a host responds on 443 but only serves a redirect or static parking page 
 a service banner suggests one technology but validation shows a different backend behavior 
 a support interface turns out to expose privileged actions

title: Subdomain Enumeration
text:
Subdomain Enumeration 
 Definition 
 Subdomain enumeration is the process of discovering subdomains associated with a target and determining which of them are live, interesting, or risky. 
 Why it matters 
 Subdomains frequently reveal alternate entry points, old environments, admin paths, vendor mappings, and ownership drift. They are one of the richest recon surfaces.
This note is narrower than general  public-asset-discovery  and earlier than full validation: its job is to expand the hostname surface before routing findings into reachability and ownership checks. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers enumerate subdomains to:
- broaden the visible asset list
- identify staging, legacy, or admin hosts
- find takeover opportunities
- discover alternate API and app entry points 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- know which subdomains are live and why
- remove stale records quickly
- classify which subdomains are public, internal, vendor-owned, or dead
- connect enumeration results to retirement and ownership reviews 
 Practical examples 
 
 enumeration reveals  staging ,  legacy-api , and  admin  hosts 
 a subdomain points to a decommissioned service and becomes a takeover risk 
 alternate customer-specific hosts expose more surface than the main domain

title: Tech Stack Fingerprinting
text:
Tech Stack Fingerprinting 
 Definition 
 Tech stack fingerprinting is the process of inferring what technologies, frameworks, CDNs, proxies, languages, or services a target is using from observable behavior and artifacts. 
 Why it matters 
 Knowing the stack changes testing strategy. It helps narrow likely route structures, auth patterns, default behaviors, proxy setups, and exposure assumptions.
Keep it distinct from  public-asset-discovery : this note is about what the target appears to be running, not simply which assets exist. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers fingerprint:
- headers and server clues
- JavaScript bundles
- route patterns
- CDN and edge behavior
- TLS and error-page hints
- public docs and asset naming 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- know what the stack leaks externally
- avoid unnecessary fingerprinting clues where sensible
- understand that stack visibility helps attackers prioritize 
 Practical examples 
 
 response headers reveal a CDN and a backend framework 
 JS bundles expose route patterns and API conventions 
 TLS and error behavior suggest a particular edge provider

title: Break JWT Validation
text:
Break JWT Validation 
 Goal 
 Identify whether JWT handling is weak enough to allow acceptance of invalid, stale, mis-scoped, or attacker-influenced tokens. 
 Assumptions 
 
 the app uses JWTs for auth or session-like state 
 validation logic may be inconsistent across services 
 developers may confuse token presence with valid authorization 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 sample tokens from authenticated flows 
 ability to replay requests 
 basic understanding of JWT structure 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Determine where tokens are issued and consumed. 
 Map claims that appear to influence authorization. 
 Identify whether multiple services validate the same token differently. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Replay expired or old tokens. 
 Remove or alter non-critical claims to observe validation strictness. 
 Test whether audience, issuer, or scope checks appear enforced. 
 Compare behavior across web, mobile, and API entry points. 
 Check whether logout or privilege changes actually revoke token usefulness. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 expired tokens still work 
 changed or missing claims are accepted 
 different services accept different token states 
 authorization decisions rely on weak or stale claims 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 validate signature, issuer, audience, expiry, and relevant claims consistently 
 separate authentication from authorization checks 
 minimize token lifetime and scope 
 design explicit revocation strategies where needed 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 repeated use of expired tokens 
 token claim anomalies 
 inconsistent auth decisions across services for same subject 
 
 Related notes 
 
  auth-flaws  
  JWT Attacks  
  token-lifecycle  
  session-management

title: Exploit IDOR
text:
Exploit IDOR 
 Goal 
 Determine whether object identifiers can be manipulated to access another user's data or actions. 
 Assumptions 
 
 the app exposes object-specific routes, IDs, or references 
 authorization might be weaker than authentication 
 the server may trust client-supplied identifiers too much 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 authenticated test accounts with different privilege scopes 
 ability to capture and replay requests 
 understanding of object locations in the app 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Map endpoints that expose object IDs in paths, query params, forms, or JSON. 
 Compare requests from two different users. 
 Look for predictable IDs, UUID reuse, sequential records, or leaked references. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Capture a request that accesses one object. 
 Change only the object identifier. 
 Replay the request with the same auth context. 
 Try both read and write operations. 
 Repeat across hidden endpoints, exports, downloads, and mobile/API routes. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 another user's object is returned 
 writes succeed against another user's resource 
 response metadata changes without an authorization error 
 timing or status changes indicate object existence 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 enforce authorization on every object access server-side 
 use indirect references only as a helper, not a replacement 
 avoid relying on UI visibility for protection 
 log authorization failures and suspicious object access patterns 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 repeated access attempts across adjacent IDs 
 many 403/404 combinations across the same route family 
 auth context accessing objects outside expected ownership scope 
 
 Related notes 
 
  broken-access-control  
  Broken Object Level Authorization  
  http-messages  
 
 References 
 
  Testing / Lab:  PortSwigger IDOR page — https://portswigger.net/web-security/access-control/idor 
  Testing / Lab:  PortSwigger access control topic — https://portswigger.net/web-security/access-control

title: Exploit SQL Injection
text:
Exploit SQL Injection 
 Goal 
 Determine whether user-controlled input can alter SQL query logic in a way that changes access, data, or behavior. 
 Assumptions 
 
 one or more endpoints send untrusted input to a database layer 
 query construction may not be safely parameterized everywhere 
 secondary features are often weaker than primary flows 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 endpoints with user-controlled input 
 ability to replay and compare requests 
 safe test environment and authorization to probe 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Map all user-controlled inputs: query params, forms, JSON, cookies, headers. 
 Look for filtering, sorting, search, exports, and admin-only features. 
 Note error behavior, timing, and content changes. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Send simple syntax probes to detect parser influence. 
 Test boolean and timing differences where appropriate. 
 Compare success/failure behavior across endpoints. 
 Probe less obvious inputs like headers, hidden params, or mobile/API routes. 
 Confirm whether the issue is read-only, auth bypass, or broader query control. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 SQL errors or stack traces 
 content differences under boolean conditions 
 measurable timing differences 
 auth or data access behavior changes unexpectedly 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 parameterize queries consistently 
 avoid raw string concatenation 
 constrain DB privileges 
 review query-building helpers and ORMs for unsafe escape hatches 
 monitor suspicious query patterns safely 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 repetitive syntax-like probes 
 abnormal query timing patterns 
 errors around query parsing or type coercion

title: Inspect File Upload Surface
text:
Inspect File Upload Surface 
 Goal 
 Determine whether upload features create unsafe execution, storage, parser, or exposure paths. 
 Assumptions 
 
 uploads may be validated weakly 
 post-processing is often riskier than the upload itself 
 storage and serving paths may cross trust boundaries 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 one or more upload or import features 
 ability to inspect storage, response behavior, or processing side effects where authorized 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Map all upload and import entry points. 
 Identify where files are stored, transformed, previewed, or served. 
 Note allowed extensions, MIME handling, naming, and public exposure. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Compare extension checks vs actual parser behavior. 
 Test whether uploaded content is served back from executable or overly trusted contexts. 
 Probe archive and document processing paths. 
 Inspect filename handling and path assumptions. 
 Look for predictable public URLs or indirect exposure of stored files. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 unsafe file types accepted or mishandled 
 uploaded content becomes publicly reachable unexpectedly 
 processing path reveals parser or storage issues 
 files can influence downstream rendering or server behavior 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 validate more than extension alone 
 isolate storage and serving paths 
 avoid execution-capable contexts 
 review previews/transforms as part of the attack surface 
 use indirect references and safe naming 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 unusual upload MIME/type combinations 
 repeated failed processing attempts 
 public access to files that should remain private 
 
 Related notes 
 
  file-upload-abuse  
  path-traversal  
  xss  
  exposed-storage  
 
 References

title: Inspect Session Handling
text:
Inspect Session Handling 
 Goal 
 Determine whether session identifiers, lifecycle, and cookie behavior are handled safely across login, logout, and privilege transitions. 
 Assumptions 
 
 the app uses cookies or token-backed session state 
 browser behavior influences risk 
 session rotation and invalidation may be incomplete 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 one or more test accounts 
 browser devtools or proxy tooling 
 ability to observe cookies and replay requests 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Record session state before login. 
 Capture Set-Cookie headers on login and logout. 
 Observe session behavior across password changes, role changes, and inactivity. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Check whether the session identifier changes after login. 
 Check whether logout invalidates server-side state. 
 Replay requests with old cookies after logout. 
 Test whether cookies use  HttpOnly ,  Secure , and appropriate  SameSite . 
 Observe whether privileged transitions reuse old session state. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 same session ID before and after login 
 old cookies still work after logout 
 cookies lack safe attributes 
 session persists unexpectedly across trust transitions 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 rotate sessions after authentication and privilege change 
 invalidate server-side sessions properly 
 set safe cookie attributes 
 keep session IDs out of URLs and logs 
 align timeout policy with risk 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 reused session IDs across multiple auth states 
 post-logout reuse of supposedly dead sessions 
 suspicious session reuse across IP/device shifts where appropriate 
 
 Related notes 
 
  session-management  
  cookies-and-sessions  
  auth-flaws  
  csrf  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  OWASP WSTG session management testing — https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/

title: Investigate SSRF
text:
Investigate SSRF 
 Goal 
 Determine whether server-side request functionality can be used to reach internal, privileged, or attacker-chosen destinations. 
 Assumptions 
 
 the app fetches URLs, files, previews, webhooks, or remote content 
 internal reachability may differ from public reachability 
 redirects and alternate representations may bypass naive validation 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 a feature that triggers server-side requests 
 ability to observe responses, timing, or side effects 
 knowledge of likely internal targets where ethically appropriate 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Identify all features that accept URLs or remote resources. 
 Observe how the server normalizes and fetches destinations. 
 Note whether redirects, DNS changes, or alternate IP formats are followed. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Confirm basic outbound request capability. 
 Test loopback, private ranges, and metadata-style targets where authorized. 
 Observe timing, status codes, header leakage, or indirect success signals. 
 Check whether redirects bypass allowlists. 
 Compare behavior for HTTP vs HTTPS and domain vs direct IP forms. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 server can fetch attacker-controlled destinations 
 internal or private targets produce distinguishable behavior 
 metadata or internal admin paths are reachable 
 URL validation blocks some forms but not equivalent alternatives 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 restrict egress destinations tightly 
 validate and normalize URLs carefully 
 avoid generic fetchers where possible 
 segment networks so app servers cannot casually reach sensitive systems 
 protect metadata access explicitly 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 unusual outbound requests from app tier 
 requests to loopback, RFC1918, or metadata-style addresses 
 failed or repeated fetch attempts against internal targets 
 
 Related notes 
 
  ssrf  
  nat-and-private-networks  
  metadata-endpoints  
  dns-resolution  
 
 References 
 
  Testing / Lab:  PortSwigger SSRF topic — https://portswigger.net/web-s

title: Reverse Proxy Misconfig Checklist
text:
Reverse Proxy Misconfig Checklist 
 Goal 
 Identify whether proxy and backend behavior create trust-boundary bugs, parser confusion, header abuse, or unintended routing exposure. 
 Assumptions 
 
 one or more reverse proxies, CDNs, or load balancers sit in front of the app 
 headers may be added, stripped, or trusted inconsistently 
 proxy/backend parsing differences can create security issues 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 visibility into requests and responses 
 ability to send crafted headers and observe behavior 
 rough understanding of the request chain 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Identify all request hops if possible. 
 Compare externally visible behavior with backend expectations. 
 Record proxy-added headers and route behaviors. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Test header trust:  X-Forwarded-For ,  X-Real-IP , host-related headers. 
 Observe whether direct backend access also exists. 
 Check routing and path normalization differences. 
 Probe for request parsing ambiguity where appropriate. 
 Inspect health, debug, or alternate virtual host exposure. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 attacker-controlled forwarding headers influence security decisions 
 proxy and backend disagree on request interpretation 
 backends are reachable directly outside the intended front door 
 hidden paths or routing behaviors appear through proxy quirks 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 define a clear trusted proxy boundary 
 normalize or reject ambiguous requests 
 restrict direct backend exposure 
 centralize and review forwarding/header trust policy 
 patch and test the whole chain consistently 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 unexpected forwarding-header values 
 malformed request patterns 
 traffic bypassing expected entrypoints 
 inconsistencies between edge and backend logs 
 
 Related notes 
 
  reverse-proxies  
  request-smuggling  
  client-ip-trust  
  load-balancers  
 
 References

title: Test Client IP Spoofing
text:
Test Client IP Spoofing 
 Goal 
 Determine whether the application or upstream components trust attacker-controlled headers when deriving the client IP. 
 Assumptions 
 
 rate limits, logs, allowlists, or admin controls may depend on client IP 
 proxies may forward original client information 
 trust settings may be broader than intended 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 a route where client IP affects behavior or is observable 
 ability to replay requests with custom headers 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Find endpoints or logs that surface perceived client IP. 
 Identify whether the app sits behind proxies or load balancers. 
 Review default framework trust-proxy behavior if known. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Send requests with crafted  X-Forwarded-For  and related headers. 
 Observe logs, rate limits, or allowlist behavior. 
 Try multi-hop header values to see how parsing is handled. 
 Compare behavior through the normal edge vs alternate paths if any. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 logs record attacker-supplied IPs 
 rate limits reset or misapply based on spoofed values 
 IP-based restrictions are bypassed 
 different services disagree on the client IP 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 trust forwarding headers only from known proxies 
 configure framework trust boundaries explicitly 
 derive security decisions from a consistent, vetted source 
 document and test the expected header chain 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 impossible or malformed forwarding chains 
 high churn in client IP for same session/account 
 mismatches between edge and app logs 
 
 Related notes 
 
  client-ip-trust  
  reverse-proxies  
  http-headers  
  API Rate Limiting  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  MDN X-Forwarded-For — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-Forwarded-For

title: Test CORS Behavior
text:
Test CORS Behavior 
 Goal 
 Determine whether CORS policy is too permissive, reflected unsafely, or misunderstood as an authorization control. 
 Assumptions 
 
 one or more API/browser endpoints rely on cross-origin access 
 the browser enforces CORS, not the server in a general auth sense 
 credentials and origin handling may be misconfigured 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 endpoints returning CORS headers 
 ability to send custom  Origin  headers 
 understanding of whether cookies or credentials are involved 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Identify cross-origin endpoints. 
 Record  Access-Control-*  headers on normal responses and preflights. 
 Observe whether origins are static, wildcard, or reflected. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Send requests with attacker-controlled  Origin  values. 
 Check whether credentials are allowed alongside reflected origins. 
 Compare preflight and non-preflight behavior. 
 Test subdomain and scheme variations of trusted origins. 
 Confirm whether the team is relying on CORS where server-side auth should exist. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 reflected attacker origin 
  Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true  with unsafe origin behavior 
 broad trust of attacker-influenceable subdomains 
 confusion between readable responses and writable state changes 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 explicitly enumerate trusted origins 
 never treat CORS as authorization 
 review credentialed cross-origin flows carefully 
 keep server-side access control independent of browser policy 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 unexpected origins hitting sensitive routes 
 drift in allowed origin patterns 
 preflight anomalies on auth-sensitive endpoints 
 
 Related notes 
 
  cors-misconfiguration  
  http-headers  
  csrf  
  cookies-and-sessions  
 
 References

title: Test Path Traversal
text:
Test Path Traversal 
 Goal 
 Determine whether user input can escape the intended filesystem path boundary and access unexpected files or directories. 
 Assumptions 
 
 the app reads or writes files based on user-controlled identifiers 
 path normalization may be incomplete 
 platform-specific separators or encodings may matter 
 
 Prerequisites 
 
 file-related endpoints or upload/extraction features 
 ability to replay requests and compare responses 
 
 Recon steps 
 
 Identify download, preview, import, extraction, and template-related features. 
 Map where path-like input appears in routes, params, or JSON. 
 Note OS/platform behavior where relevant. 
 
 Exploit / test steps 
 
 Try simple traversal sequences. 
 Test encoded or alternate separator variants where appropriate. 
 Compare behavior for read and write operations. 
 Probe archive extraction or upload workflows for directory escape. 
 Observe whether normalization happens before or after validation. 
 
 Validation clues 
 
 access to unexpected files 
 different errors when escaping intended directories 
 writes or extractions landing outside controlled paths 
 
 Mitigation 
 
 avoid raw user-controlled filesystem paths 
 use indirect references or strict allowlists 
 canonicalize safely and verify final paths 
 isolate processing and storage accounts/dirs 
 
 Logging / detection 
 
 repeated traversal-like strings 
 failed file access outside expected trees 
 extraction or write attempts outside upload areas 
 
 Related notes

  file-upload-abuse  
  http-messages  
  exposed-storage  
 
 References

title: Authentication Flaws
text:
Authentication Flaws 
 Definition 
 Authentication flaws are weaknesses in how an application verifies identity. They include weak login logic, user enumeration, broken MFA flows, password reset weaknesses, credential stuffing exposure, and flawed assumptions around session/token issuance. 
 Why it matters 
 Authentication is the front door to the app. When it fails, attackers often gain access before needing anything more advanced. These flaws also expand the attack surface for privilege escalation and account takeover. 
 This note is about proving identity correctly.  session-management  covers what happens after login succeeds, and  JWT Attacks  covers token-specific failure modes rather than the broader auth flow. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- login error behavior
- brute-force protections
- MFA enforcement gaps
- password reset and recovery flows
- token issuance after partial validation
- user enumeration through timing or messages 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- make authentication flows explicit and testable
- limit brute-force and credential stuffing impact
- harden password reset and recovery
- treat MFA as a control with edge cases, not a magic layer
- avoid leaking account existence or auth state 
 Practical examples 
 
 The app reveals whether an email exists at login. 
 Password reset tokens are weak or never expire. 
 MFA is enforced in the UI but not on a direct API call. 
 
 Related notes 
 
  session-management  
  JWT Attacks  
  broken-access-control  
  client-ip-trust  
  cookies-and-sessions  
 
 References 
 
  Foundational:  OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet — https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html

title: Broken Access Control
text:
Broken Access Control 
 Definition 
 Broken access control happens when an application fails to enforce what a user is allowed to access or do. This includes horizontal privilege escalation, vertical privilege escalation, insecure direct object access, and hidden functionality that is reachable without proper authorization. 
 Why it matters 
 This is one of the most dangerous classes of web vulnerabilities because it often leads directly to unauthorized data access or privileged actions. It also appears in mature applications because teams frequently implement authentication but underinvest in authorization. 
 This note is the umbrella access-control note.  idor  is the web object-reference pattern inside that broader category, while  Broken Object Level Authorization  is the API-specific object-level variant. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test access control by changing:
- object identifiers
- routes
- HTTP methods
- hidden parameters
- role assumptions
- step order in a workflow 
 They look for places where the server trusts the client too much or fails to re-check authorization at the object and action level. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should separate:
- authentication: who are you?
- authorization: what can you access or do? 
 Every sensitive action and object lookup should be enforced server-side, not implied by UI visibility. 
 Practical examples 
 
 Changing  /account/123  to  /account/124  reveals another user's data. 
 A normal user can call an admin-only POST endpoint directly. 
 The frontend hides a button, but the backend still accepts the request. 
 
 Related notes 
 
  auth-flaws  
  session-management  
  idor  
  Broken Object Level Authorization  
  business-logic-vulnerabilities  
 
 References

  Foundational:  OWASP WSTG authorization testing — https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_App

title: Business Logic Vulnerabilities
text:
Business Logic Vulnerabilities 
 Definition 
 Business logic vulnerabilities are flaws in the intended workflow or rules of an application rather than low-level parser or interpreter bugs. The app behaves “as coded” but not “as safely intended”. 
 Why it matters 
 These vulnerabilities are common in mature products because they exploit assumptions about sequence, pricing, authorization context, reuse, or invariants rather than obvious technical mistakes. They are also where understanding the product deeply becomes a security advantage. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- step ordering
- repeatability of one-time actions
- discount or credit logic
- workflow branching
- race conditions in business actions
- assumptions that the UI enforces but the backend never validates 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- model abuse cases, not just happy paths
- define invariants at the server side
- test sequences and edge cases intentionally
- log unusual workflow behavior
- understand that business rules themselves are part of the attack surface 
 Practical examples 
 
 A coupon can be reused because the invalidation step happens too late. 
 A checkout workflow allows price manipulation across steps. 
 A user can move to a privileged workflow stage by calling the endpoint out of order. 
 
 Related notes 
 
  broken-access-control  
  auth-flaws  
  request-smuggling  
  attack-surface-mapping  
 
 References

  Testing / Lab:  PortSwigger Web Security Academy — https://portswigger.net/web-security

title: CORS Misconfiguration
text:
CORS Misconfiguration 
 Definition 
 CORS misconfiguration happens when cross-origin resource sharing policies are too permissive, incorrectly implemented, or misunderstood. CORS is a browser-enforced policy layer, not a general-purpose server-side access control mechanism. 
 Why it matters 
 CORS is frequently misunderstood by developers. Teams often treat it as an authorization control when it is really about which browser origins can read responses under specific conditions. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- reflected origins
- wildcard behavior with credentials
- trust of subdomains or attacker-controlled origins
- confusion between CORS and CSRF
- preflight handling edge cases 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- treat CORS as a browser policy, not auth
- explicitly define trusted origins
- understand credentialed requests
- avoid reflecting arbitrary origins
- review how CORS interacts with cookies and APIs 
 Practical examples 
 
 The server reflects the  Origin  header and sets  Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true . 
 An internal API trusts a broad subdomain pattern that an attacker can influence. 
 A team assumes “CORS blocks attackers”, while the real risk is still CSRF or server-side abuse. 
 
 Related notes 
 
  http-headers  
  csrf  
  cookies-and-sessions  
  auth-flaws  
  Test CORS Behavior  
 
 References

title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
text:
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) 
 Definition 
 CSRF happens when a victim’s browser is tricked into sending an authenticated request that the target application accepts as legitimate. It depends on browser behavior, session state, and insufficient anti-CSRF protections. 
 Why it matters 
 CSRF teaches an important lesson: the browser can be an involuntary deputy. If the app relies on automatically sent credentials without properly validating request origin or intent, attackers can cause actions without reading the response. 
 This note stays distinct from  cors-misconfiguration : CORS controls which origins may read responses in the browser, while CSRF is about making the browser send authenticated requests in the first place. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- state-changing endpoints
- cookie-based auth
- missing anti-CSRF tokens
- weak origin/referer validation
- unsafe use of GET for actions
- CORS misconceptions 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- use anti-CSRF tokens where needed
- understand when SameSite helps and when it does not solve everything
- validate origin where appropriate
- keep state-changing operations off unsafe request patterns
- understand which auth mechanisms are automatically sent by the browser 
 Practical examples 
 
 A logged-in user visits an attacker page that silently submits a POST form to the target app. 
 A settings endpoint changes state on GET. 
 An application assumes CORS configuration alone prevents CSRF.

  session-management  
  cors-misconfiguration  
  http-overview  
 
 References

title: File Upload Abuse
text:
File Upload Abuse 
 Definition 
 File upload abuse happens when an application accepts or processes uploaded files in unsafe ways. The risk is not only “uploading a script”; it also includes parser exploitation, path confusion, storage exposure, content-type confusion, and dangerous post-processing. 
 Why it matters 
 Uploads are a rich attack surface because they often cross multiple trust boundaries:
- browser → server
- filesystem or object storage
- preview or transformation pipeline
- downstream consumers 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- extension and MIME validation
- parser behavior
- archive extraction
- image/document processing
- upload-to-execution paths
- public storage exposure and predictable naming 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- validate by more than extension alone
- isolate storage and processing paths
- avoid direct execution or serving from unsafe contexts
- scan and transform cautiously
- think about what happens after upload, not just at upload time 
 Practical examples 
 
 A file upload endpoint only checks extension. 
 Uploaded files are stored under a web-accessible path and can be requested directly. 
 Archive extraction writes outside the intended directory. 
 
 Related notes 
 
  path-traversal  
  xss  
  business-logic-vulnerabilities  
  exposed-storage  
  Inspect File Upload Surface  
 
 References

title: IDOR
text:
IDOR 
 Definition 
 Insecure Direct Object Reference is a web-access pattern where an attacker changes an object identifier in a request and the application returns or modifies another user's data because the server does not enforce object-level authorization correctly. 
 Why It Matters 
 IDOR is one of the clearest ways broken access control becomes obvious in real applications. It frequently appears in browser-backed CRUD flows, download routes, profile pages, and other object-centric web features. 
 This note is the web-facing object-reference pattern.  Broken Object Level Authorization  is the API-side note for the same underlying failure when object authorization is expressed through API resources and tenant-scoped data models. 
 Attacker Perspective 
 Attackers capture a valid request, change only the object reference, and look for unauthorized reads, writes, or existence leaks. They often test predictable IDs, hidden links, export routes, and object references exposed in forms or client-side code. 
 Defender Perspective 
 Defenders should treat every object lookup as an authorization decision, not just a data fetch. UI visibility, obfuscated identifiers, and route hiding do not replace server-side ownership or policy checks. 
 Practical Examples 
 
 Changing  /account/123  to  /account/124  reveals another user's profile. 
 A download endpoint accepts  invoiceId=9321  and returns another customer's PDF. 
 A frontend hides edit controls, but replaying the underlying request still updates another user's record. 
 
 Related Notes

  Broken Object Level Authorization  
  HTTP Messages  
  Exploit IDOR  
  Vulnerable Web App Project

title: OWASP Top 10
text:
OWASP Top 10 
 Definition 
 The OWASP Top 10 is a high-level awareness document that summarizes the most important web application security risk categories. It is not a complete methodology, but it is one of the best starting maps for what repeatedly goes wrong in real applications. 
 Why it matters 
 This note matters because it helps organize the web-security branch around root causes instead of random exploits. It gives you a vocabulary for common risk categories and a shared frame for discussing priorities, testing, and mitigation. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers do not care about the Top 10 as a checklist. They care about the underlying classes of weakness it names:
- broken access control
- injection
- insecure design
- identification and authentication failures
- security misconfiguration 
 The value of this note is not memorization. It is learning where entire families of exploitable mistakes come from. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should use the Top 10 as:
- a prioritization aid
- a communication tool with developers
- a bridge into deeper standards like WSTG and ASVS 
 It is useful for orientation, but not enough by itself to test or secure an application. 
 Practical examples 
 
 A team says “we don’t have SQL injection,” but still has broken access control and insecure design. 
 A review names “XSS” as the problem, but the root cause is broader input/output handling and missing security controls. 
 A roadmap uses Top 10 categories to decide which labs and playbooks to build first. 
 
 Related notes 
 
  broken-access-control  
  auth-flaws  
  sql-injection  
  xss  
  ssrf  
 
 References

title: Path Traversal
text:
Path Traversal 
 Definition 
 Path traversal happens when user-controlled input influences filesystem paths in a way that allows access outside the intended directory. This often uses  ../  sequences or equivalent encoding tricks to escape the expected path boundary. 
 Why it matters 
 This vulnerability is a clean example of unsafe trust between user input and sensitive server-side resources. It also teaches how normalization and canonicalization mistakes can lead to exposure. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- file download endpoints
- image/document retrieval
- archive extraction paths
- template or include paths
- encoded traversal sequences
- platform-specific separators and normalization quirks 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- never let raw user input directly build filesystem paths
- use allowlists or indirect references
- canonicalize and verify paths safely
- contain file access to dedicated directories and accounts
- review archive extraction and upload handling carefully 
 Practical examples 
 
  GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd  
 A document previewer reads arbitrary files from the host 
 A zip extraction feature writes outside the intended upload directory 
 
 Related notes 
 
  file-upload-abuse  
  Test Path Traversal  
  reverse-proxies  
  http-messages  
 
 References

title: Request Smuggling
text:
Request Smuggling 
 Definition 
 Request smuggling happens when two components in the request chain parse the same HTTP request differently. This often occurs between front-end and back-end servers when they disagree about message boundaries. 
 Why it matters 
 This is one of the clearest examples of networking and web security colliding. It depends on HTTP message parsing, proxy behavior, and trust assumptions between components. The impact can include request hijacking, cache poisoning, auth bypass side effects, and internal routing confusion. 
 This note is specifically about parser disagreement across the request chain. It should stay tied to  http-messages  and  reverse-proxies  rather than expanding into a generic note about every proxy misconfiguration. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- inconsistent handling of  Content-Length  and  Transfer-Encoding 
- front-end/back-end parser differences
- hidden request queue behavior
- cache and routing side effects
- whether one request can interfere with another user’s request stream 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- understand their exact request chain
- ensure components agree on parsing behavior
- normalize or reject ambiguous requests
- patch and test proxies and backends consistently
- treat HTTP parsing as a security boundary, not just infrastructure detail 
 Practical examples 
 
 A reverse proxy and backend disagree on which bytes belong to the next request. 
 One user’s crafted request influences how the next request is interpreted. 
 A hidden admin path becomes reachable via parser confusion.

title: Session Management
text:
Session Management 
 Definition 
 Session management is the set of mechanisms an application uses to maintain authenticated state across requests. This includes session creation, rotation, expiration, invalidation, and how session identifiers are transported and protected. 
 Why it matters 
 Even when authentication is correct, weak session management can undermine the entire security model. Sessions sit at the intersection of browser behavior, cookies, auth logic, and server-side trust. 
 This note is about maintaining authenticated state safely after identity has been established.  auth-flaws  covers login and identity proof failures, while  JWT Attacks  covers token-specific state and validation failures. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- predictable or reusable session identifiers
- missing rotation after login or privilege change
- weak logout behavior
- fixation opportunities
- cookies with unsafe attributes
- session leakage through URLs or logs 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- rotate session identifiers at meaningful trust transitions
- expire sessions appropriately
- invalidate server-side state correctly
- keep session identifiers out of URLs
- apply secure cookie attributes and consistent server-side checks 
 Practical examples 
 
 A user logs in but keeps the same session identifier from before authentication. 
 Logout clears the browser cookie but leaves the server-side session valid. 
 Sessions survive password changes or role transitions without revalidation.

title: SQL Injection
text:
SQL Injection 
 Definition 
 SQL injection happens when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL in a way that changes the intended query logic. The classic impact is unauthorized data access, but it can also lead to authentication bypass, data modification, or deeper system compromise depending on the environment. 
 Why it matters 
 SQL injection remains one of the clearest examples of server-side trust failure. It teaches the core security lesson that data and code must remain separate, especially at interpreter boundaries. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers probe:
- query parameters
- form fields
- JSON bodies
- cookies
- headers
- hidden or secondary request paths 
 They look for syntax breaks, Boolean behavior changes, timing differences, and error messages that reveal the query context. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- use parameterized queries consistently
- avoid string concatenation in query construction
- constrain privileges at the database layer
- validate and normalize input without treating validation as the primary defense
- log suspicious query behavior safely 
 Practical examples 
 
 A login form concatenates the username and password into raw SQL. 
 A reporting endpoint uses a sortable field directly in a query expression. 
 An internal admin feature is vulnerable because “trusted” users were assumed safe. 
 
 Related notes

  business-logic-vulnerabilities  
  http-messages

title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
text:
Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) 
 Definition 
 SSRF happens when an application can be induced to make a server-side request to an attacker-chosen destination. The key danger is that the server can often reach internal or privileged resources that the external attacker cannot directly access. 
 Why it matters 
 SSRF is one of the best examples of why networking and web security must be learned together. It turns server-side network reachability into exploitable impact. 
 This note stays focused on the web exploit pattern. The networking branch carries the reachability context:  nat-and-private-networks ,  metadata-endpoints ,  dns-resolution , and  reverse-proxies  explain why the same fetch bug can have very different blast radius in different environments. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers look for:
- URL fetchers
- image importers
- webhook testers
- PDF/rendering tools
- integration preview features
- services that resolve or request arbitrary URLs 
 They try to pivot into:
- internal admin services
- private APIs
- metadata endpoints
- loopback-only services 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- restrict where server-side requests can go
- validate and normalize destinations carefully
- understand redirects, DNS rebinding, and alternate IP notations
- segment networks so the app cannot casually reach sensitive internals
- treat SSRF as both an application and infrastructure problem 
 Practical examples 
 
 A URL preview feature fetches arbitrary addresses. 
 A backend can reach  169.254.169.254  and expose cloud metadata. 
 An internal-only service becomes reachable through a public app’s fetch capability.

  metadata-endpoints  
  dns-resolution  
  reverse-proxies

title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
text:
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) 
 Definition 
 XSS happens when untrusted input is included in a web page or browser-executed context in a way that allows attacker-controlled JavaScript to run. It can be reflected, stored, or DOM-based depending on where the unsafe handling occurs. 
 Why it matters 
 XSS is not just “alert(1)”. It turns a browser into an execution environment under attacker influence. That can lead to session theft, action forgery, UI redressing, data exfiltration, and pivoting into other application functionality. 
 Attacker perspective 
 Attackers test:
- reflected inputs
- stored content fields
- rich text or markdown features
- query parameters rendered into the DOM
- JavaScript sinks and templating mistakes 
 They care about context: HTML, attribute, script, URL, CSS, or DOM sink. 
 Defender perspective 
 Defenders should:
- output-encode by context
- sanitize only where appropriate and carefully
- avoid unsafe DOM sinks
- use CSP as a mitigation layer, not a substitute for correct handling
- assume XSS can amplify the impact of many other bugs 
 Practical examples 
 
 A search term is reflected unsafely into HTML. 
 A comment field stores attacker input that every admin later renders. 
 A frontend script takes a query parameter and injects it directly into  innerHTML . 
 
 Related notes 
 
  session-management  
  HTTP Headers  
  csrf  
 
 References

  Foundational:  OWASP Cheat Sheet Series — https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/

title: Projects Index
text:
Projects Index 
 Purpose 
 This is the integration layer of the cybersecurity vault. 
 Projects are where branch knowledge becomes practical work. 
 A project should:
- combine multiple branches
- exercise real attack surface or control design
- connect concept notes to playbooks
- create reusable experience, not just theory 
 Projects are not just “examples”.
They are structured learning and testing environments. 
 
 How to use projects 
 For each project:
1. identify the main branches involved
2. review the key concept notes first
3. use the relevant playbooks during execution
4. record findings, gaps, and follow-up notes
5. link back to canonical notes instead of duplicating theory

 Architecture / System Shape 
 
 API gateway or reverse proxy 
 REST API service 
 PostgreSQL or similar store 
 Redis for distributed rate limits 
 Structured application logging 
 
 Attack Surface 
 
 Auth and token refresh endpoints 
 Role-protected administrative routes 
 Object identifiers in paths and bodies 
 Undocumented or deprecated API versions 
 URL-fetching or webhook features that may trigger  SSRF  
 
 Testing Hypotheses 
 
 Missing per-object checks allow  BOLA . 
 Role-only gates miss function-level auth bypasses. 
 Weak token validation or replay window creates session abuse. 
 Missing inventory control leaves old endpoints reachable. 
 Unbounded requests enable abuse or brute force. 
 
 Findings 
 
 Fill during testing. 
 
 Fixes / Hardening Decisions 
 
 Centralized auth middleware 
 Policy-driven authorization 
 Redis-backed limits 
 Inventory/version ownership 
 Safer token issue, rotation, and revocation logic
